chunks.heading: 5. What exactly were the consequences in the environment?
chunks.preview: General Worksheet 2A (p. 3 of 3) 5. What exactly were the consequences in the environment? Short-term: I had to spend the night in jail. Long-term: My boyfriend has less trust in me; my sister is upset about it. And in myself? Short-term: I am ashamed and furious with myself. Long-term: I will have...
chunks.text: General Worksheet 2A (p. 3 of 3) 5. What exactly were the consequences in the environment? Short-term: I had to spend the night in jail. Long-term: My boyfriend has less trust in me; my sister is upset about it. And in myself? Short-term: I am ashamed and furious with myself. Long-term: I will have to pay more for car insurance and may have trouble getting a job. What harm did my problem behavior cause? It hurt me by giving me a DUI record. My sister feels guilty because she upset me. 7. Prevention plans: Ways to reduce my vulnerability in the future: Make plans for how to cope whenever my boyfriend is out of town. Ways to prevent precipitating event from happening again: I canвҖҷt keep the precipitating event from happening, so I need to practice coping ahead and have plans for how to manage when I am at home alone. 8. Plans to repair, correct, and overcorrect the harm: Apologize to my sister and reassure her that she has a perfect right to change her plans. Work with her to plan a new time for a visit. Ask if it would be easier for her if I came to visit her.

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Skills
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Skills Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets Mindfulness is the act of consciously focusing the mind in the present moment, without judgment and without attachment to the moment. A person who is mindful is aware in and of the present moment. Mindfulness is the opposite of being on вҖңaut...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Skills Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets Mindfulness is the act of consciously focusing the mind in the present moment, without judgment and without attachment to the moment. A person who is mindful is aware in and of the present moment. Mindfulness is the opposite of being on вҖңautomatic pilot,вҖқ or being lost in habit. Mindfulness has to do with the quality of awareness that a person brings to everyday living. ItвҖҷs a way of living awake, with eyes wide open. As a set of skills, mindfulness practice is the intentional process of observing, describing, and participating in reality nonjudgmentally, in the moment, and with effectiveness (i.e., using skillful means). We can contrast mindfulness with rigidly clinging to the present moment, as if we could keep a present moment from changing if we cling hard enough. When we are mindful, we are open to the fluidity of each moment as it arises and falls away. Goals and Definitions вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 1: Goals of Mindfulness Practice. The goals of practicing mindfulness skills, for most people, are to reduce suffering, increase happiness, and increase control of the mind. For some, a goal of mindfulness is to experience reality as it is. Mindfulness skills require practice, practice, practice. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 2: Mindfulness Definitions. This handout offers basic definitions of mindfulness, mindfulness skills, and mindfulness practice. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheet 1: Pros and Cons of Practicing Mindfulness. This worksheet is designed to help you decide whether you have anything to gain from practicing mindfulness. Core Mindfulness Skills The handouts and worksheets for Core Mindfulness Skills cover seven skills in three sets: Wise Mind; the вҖңwhatвҖқ skills of observing, describing, and participating; and the вҖңhowвҖқ skills of practicing nonjudgmentally, one-Вӯmindfully, and effectively.

chunks.heading: 40 вҖў Mindfulness Skills
chunks.preview: 40 вҖў Mindfulness Skills вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheets 2, 2a, 2b, and 2c: Mindfulness Core Skills Practice offer four variations for recording practice of all seven core mindfulness skills. They can be useful for recording practice after you have learned all of the core skills. Mindfulness Worksheet 2c:...
chunks.text: 40 вҖў Mindfulness Skills вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheets 2, 2a, 2b, and 2c: Mindfulness Core Skills Practice offer four variations for recording practice of all seven core mindfulness skills. They can be useful for recording practice after you have learned all of the core skills. Mindfulness Worksheet 2c: Mindfulness Core Skills Calendar offers a calendar format for recording practice of all these skills. Wise Mind вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 3: Wise Mind: States of Mind. Wise Mind is the inner wisdom that each one of us has. When we access our inner wisdom, we say we are in Wise Mind. When we enter the state of Wise Mind, we integrate oppositesвҖ”Вӯ including our reasonable and emotional states of mindвҖ”and we are open to experiencing reality as it is. вҖўвҖў You can record your practice efforts on Mindfulness Worksheet 3: Wise Mind Practice. (Mindfulness Handout 3a: Ideas for Practicing Wise Mind offers practice ideas.) Worksheet 3 asks you to rate how effective your practice was in accessing your own Wise Mind. Note that the rating is not about whether the practice calmed you or made you feel better. Mindfulness вҖңWhatвҖқ Skills вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 4: Taking Hold of Your Mind: вҖңWhatвҖқ Skills. вҖңWhatвҖқ skills are what you do when practicing mindfulnessвҖ”Вӯobserve, describe, or participate. Do only one of these activities at a time. To observe is to pay attention on purpose to the present moment. To describe is to put into words what you have observed. To participate is to enter into an activity fully and wholly, becoming one with whatever you are doing. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 4a: Ideas for Practicing Observing, Mindfulness Handout 4b: Ideas for Practicing Describing, and Mindfulness Handout 4c: Ideas for Practicing Participating offer ideas for how to practice each of the mindfulness вҖңwhatвҖқ skills. If you are just learning these skills, your skills trainer is likely to assign a specific exercise or two after you first practice each skill in a session. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheets 4, 4a, and 4b offer three different formats for recording practice of mindfulness вҖңwhatвҖқ skills. Worksheet 4 provides space for practice of the вҖңwhatвҖқ skills only twice between sessions. Worksheet 4a gives space for multiple practices for each вҖңwhatвҖқ skill in a checklist format. Worksheet 4b is aimed at those who like to write describing their practice. Mindfulness вҖңHowвҖқ Skills вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 5: Taking Hold of Your Mind: вҖңHowвҖқ Skills. The вҖңhowвҖқ skills are how you observe, describe, or participateвҖ”Вӯnonjudgmentally, one-Вӯ mindfully, and effectively. Although the вҖңwhatвҖқ skills should only be done one at a time, the вҖңhowвҖқ skills can be done together.

chunks.heading: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 41
chunks.preview: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 41 вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 5a: Ideas for Practicing Nonjudgmentalness, MindВӯfulВӯ ness Handout 5b: Ideas for Practicing One-ВӯMindfulness, and Mindfulness Handout 5c: Ideas for Practicing Effectiveness offer ideas for how to practice each of the mindfulness вҖңho...
chunks.text: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 41 вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 5a: Ideas for Practicing Nonjudgmentalness, MindВӯfulВӯ ness Handout 5b: Ideas for Practicing One-ВӯMindfulness, and Mindfulness Handout 5c: Ideas for Practicing Effectiveness offer ideas for how to practice each of the mindfulness вҖңhowвҖқ skills. If you are just learning these skills, your skills trainer is likely to assign a specific exercise or two after you practice each one in a session. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheet 5: Mindfulness вҖңHowвҖқ Skills: Nonjudgmentalness, One-Mindfulness, Effectiveness provides space for recording only two practices of a вҖңhowвҖқ skill for the week. Mindfulness Worksheet 5a: Nonjudgmentalness, One-ВӯMindfulness, Effectiveness Checklist offers a checklist format for recording вҖңhowвҖқ skills practice, and Mindfulness Worksheet 5b: Nonjudgmentalness, One-Вӯ Mindfulness, Effectiveness Calendar offers a calendar format for this purpose. Mindfulness Worksheet 5c: Nonjudgmentalness Calendar is an advanced worksheet for the single skill of nonjudgmentalness. Other Perspectives on Mindfulness Skills There are three sets of handouts and worksheets for mindfulness skills that give a different perspective on mindfulness. These are Mindfulness Practice: A Spiritual Perspective; Skillful Means: Balancing Doing Mind and Being Mind; and Wise Mind: Walking the Middle Path. Some DBT skills training programs may include one or more of these sets of skills. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 6: Overview: Other Perspectives on Mindfulness. This handout briefly previews the three supplementary mindfulness skills. Mindfulness Practice: A Spiritual Perspective вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 7: Goals of Mindfulness Practice: A Spiritual Perspective. Mindfulness can be practiced for psychological reasons or spiritual reasons. A spiritual perspective on mindfulness is included for those for whom spirituality is an important part of their life. Mindfulness practice is very old, arising initially from spiritual practices across many cultures, and it has a modern-day presence in many contemplative prayer and meditation practices. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 7a: Wise Mind from a Spiritual Perspective. This handout outlines different types of spiritual practices and includes some of the many terms used to reference the transcendent. Many spiritual and religious practices share elements in common with mindfulness practices, including silence, quieting the mind, attentiveness, inwardness, and receptivity. These are characteristics of deep spiritual experiences. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 8: Practicing Loving Kindness to Increase Love and Compassion. Anger, hate, hostility, and ill will toward ourselves and toward others can be very painful. The practice of loving kindness is a form of meditation in which specific positive words and phrases are repeatedly recited, to cultivate compassion and loving feelings as an antidote to negativity. Loving kindness is an ancient spiritual meditation practice. In some ways it is similar to praying for the welfare of

chunks.heading: 42 вҖў Mindfulness Skills
chunks.preview: 42 вҖў Mindfulness Skills ourselves and others. To record practice of loving kindness, use Mindfulness Worksheet 6: Loving Kindness, which provides space for describing two occasions of practicing loving kindness. Skillful Means: Balancing Doing Mind and Being Mind вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 9: Skillful M...
chunks.text: 42 вҖў Mindfulness Skills ourselves and others. To record practice of loving kindness, use Mindfulness Worksheet 6: Loving Kindness, which provides space for describing two occasions of practicing loving kindness. Skillful Means: Balancing Doing Mind and Being Mind вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 9: Skillful Means: Balancing Doing Mind and Being Mind. вҖңSkillful meansвҖқ is a term in Zen that refers to any effective method that aids you to experience reality as it isвҖ”or, in DBT terms, to enter fully into Wise Mind. Doing mind and being mind are states of mind that, in their extreme forms, can get in the way of skillful means and of Wise Mind. Doing mind focuses on achieving goals; being mind focuses on experiencing. The polarity between them is similar to that between reasonable mind and emotion mind. In everyday life, wise living requires us to balance working to achieve goals (on the one hand), and at the very same time to let go of attachment to achieving goals (on the other hand). вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 9a: Ideas for Practicing Balancing Doing Mind and Being Mind. This handout lists practice exercises. It is useful when you have already gone through mindfulness training several times. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheet 7a: Mindfulness of Being and Doing Calendar, Mindfulness Worksheet 8: Mindfulness of Pleasant Events Calendar, and Mindfulness Worksheet 9: Mindfulness of Unpleasant Events Calendar are all worksheets in calendar format that ask participants to record their mindfulness practice each day. The calendars focus on mindfulness during frazzled moments (Worksheet 7a), pleasant events (Worksheet 8), and unpleasant events (Worksheet 9). Wise Mind: Walking the Middle Path вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Handout 10: Walking the Middle Path: Finding the Synthesis between Opposites. Wise Mind is the middle path between extremes. In Wise Mind, we replace вҖңeitherвҖ“orвҖқ with вҖңbothвҖ“andвҖқ thinking in an effort to find a synthesis between oppositions. Ordinarily, when we are at an extreme on any continuum, we are in danger of distorting reality. This handout is useful if you have already gone through mindfulness training one or more times. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheet 10: Walking the Middle Path to Wise Mind. This worksheet lists several polarities that could be out of balance, and provides space for recording practice aimed at balancing them. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheet 10a: Analyzing Yourself on the Middle Path. Use this worksheet to think through whether you are out of balance on each of the polarities listed. вҖңOut of balanceвҖқ here means a living style that knocks you off your center, out of Wise Mind. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness Worksheet 10b: Walking the Middle Path Calendar. This worksheet offers opportunities for recording daily practice in a different format than in Worksheet 10. It can also be used in conjunction with Worksheet 10a.

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 1a
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 1a Mindfulness Definitions What Is Mindfulness? вҖўвҖў Intentionally living with awareness in the present moment. (Waking up from automatic or rote behaviors to participate and be present to our own lives.) вҖўвҖў Without judging or rejecting the moment. (Noticing consequences, discernin...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 1a Mindfulness Definitions What Is Mindfulness? вҖўвҖў Intentionally living with awareness in the present moment. (Waking up from automatic or rote behaviors to participate and be present to our own lives.) вҖўвҖў Without judging or rejecting the moment. (Noticing consequences, discerning helpfulness and harmfulnessвҖ”but letting go of evaluating, avoiding, suppressing, or blocking the present moment.) вҖўвҖў Without attachment to the moment. (Attending to the experience of each new moment, rather than ignoring the present by clinging to the past or grabbing for the future.) What Are Mindfulness Skills? вҖўвҖў Mindfulness skills are the specific behaviors to practice that, when put together, make up mindfulness. What Is Mindfulness Practice? вҖўвҖў Mindfulness and mindfulness skills can be practiced at any time, anywhere, while doing anything. Intentionally paying attention to the moment, without judging it or holding on to it, is all that is needed. вҖўвҖў Meditation is practicing mindfulness and mindfulness skills while sitting, standing, or lying quietly for a predetermined period of time. When meditating, we focus the mind (for example, we focus on body sensations, emotions, thoughts, or our breath), or we open the mind (paying attention to whatever comes into our awareness). There are many forms of meditation that differ mostly by whether we are opening the mind or focusing the mindвҖ”and, if focusing, depending on what is the focus of our attention. вҖўвҖў Contemplative prayer (such as Christian centering prayer, the rosary, Jewish Shema, Islamic Sufi practice, or Hindu raja yoga) is a spiritual mindfulness practice. вҖўвҖў Mindfulness movement also has many forms. Examples include yoga, martial arts (such as Qigong, tai chi, akido, and karate), and spiritual dancing. Hiking, horseback riding, and walking can also be ways to practice mindfulness. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: (Mindfulness Worksheet 3; p. 83)
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 3a (p. 1 of 2) (Mindfulness Worksheet 3; p. 83) Ideas for Practicing Wise Mind The mindfulness skills often require a lot of practice. As with any new skill, it is important to first practice when you donвҖҷt need the skill. If you practice in easier situations, the skill will beco...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 3a (p. 1 of 2) (Mindfulness Worksheet 3; p. 83) Ideas for Practicing Wise Mind The mindfulness skills often require a lot of practice. As with any new skill, it is important to first practice when you donвҖҷt need the skill. If you practice in easier situations, the skill will become automatic, and you will have the skill when you need it. Practice with your eyes closed and with your eyes open. 1. пҒұ Stone flake on the lake. Imagine that you are by a clear blue lake on a beautiful sunny day. Then imagine that you are a small flake of stone, flat and light. Imagine that you have been tossed out onto the lake and are now gently, slowly, floating through the calm, clear blue water to the lakeвҖҷs smooth, sandy bottom. вҖўвҖў Notice what you see, what you feel as you float down, perhaps in slow circles, floating toward the bottom. As you reach the bottom of the lake, settle your attention there within yourself. вҖўвҖў Notice the serenity of the lake; become aware of the calmness and quiet deep within. вҖўвҖў As you reach the center of your self, settle your attention there. Walking down the spiral stairs. Imagine that within you is a spiral staircase, winding down 2. пҒұ to your very center. Starting at the top walk very slowly down the staircase, going deeper and deeper within yourself. вҖўвҖў Notice the sensations. Rest by sitting on a step, or turn on lights on the way down if you wish. Do not force yourself further than you want to go. Notice the quiet. As you reach the center of your self, settle your attention thereвҖ”Вӯperhaps in your gut or your abdomen. Breathing вҖңWiseвҖқ in, вҖңMindвҖқ out. Breathing in, say to yourself, вҖңWiseвҖқ; breathing out, say 3. пҒұ вҖңMind.вҖқ вҖўвҖў Focus your entire attention on the word вҖңwise,вҖқ then, focus it again entirely on the word вҖңmind.вҖқ вҖўвҖў Continue until you sense that you have settled into Wise Mind. Asking Wise Mind a question. Breathing in, silently ask Wise Mind a question. 4. пҒұ вҖўвҖў Breathing out, listen for the answer. вҖўвҖў Listen, but do not give yourself the answer. Do not tell yourself the answer; listen for it. вҖўвҖў Continue asking on each in-Вӯbreath for some time. If no answer comes, try again another time. (continued on next page) From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Asking is this Wise Mind? Breathing in, ask yourself, вҖңIs this (action, thought, plan, etc.)
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 3a (p. 2 of 2) Asking is this Wise Mind? Breathing in, ask yourself, вҖңIs this (action, thought, plan, etc.) 5. пҒұ Wise Mind?вҖқ вҖўвҖў Breathing out, listen for the answer. вҖўвҖў Listen, but do not give yourself the answer. Do not tell yourself the answer; listen for it. вҖўвҖў Continue asking...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 3a (p. 2 of 2) Asking is this Wise Mind? Breathing in, ask yourself, вҖңIs this (action, thought, plan, etc.) 5. пҒұ Wise Mind?вҖқ вҖўвҖў Breathing out, listen for the answer. вҖўвҖў Listen, but do not give yourself the answer. Do not tell yourself the answer; listen for it. вҖўвҖў Continue asking on each in-Вӯbreath for some time. If no answer comes, try again another time. Attending to your breath coming in and out, let your attention settle into your center. 6. пҒұ вҖўвҖў Breathing in completely, notice and follow the sensations of your breath coming in. вҖўвҖў Let your attention settle into your center, at the bottom of your breath, at your solar plexusвҖ”or вҖўвҖў Let your attention settle in the center of your forehead, your вҖңthird eye,вҖқ at the top of your breath. вҖўвҖў Keeping your attention at your center, exhale, breathing normally, maintaining attention. вҖўвҖў Settle into Wise Mind. Expanding awareness. Breathing in, focus your awareness on your center. 7. пҒұ вҖўвҖў Breathing out, stay aware of your center, but expand awareness to the space you are in now. вҖўвҖў Continue on in the moment. Dropping into the pauses between inhaling and exhaling. 8. пҒұ вҖўвҖў Breathing in, notice the pause after inhaling (top of breath). вҖўвҖў Breathing out, notice the pause after exhaling (bottom of breath). вҖўвҖў At each pause, let yourself вҖңfall intoвҖқ the center space within the pause. Other Wise Mind practice ideas: 9. пҒұ

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 4
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 4 (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 4вҖ“4b; pp. 78вҖ“82, 84вҖ“87) В« Taking Hold of Your Mind: вҖңWhatвҖқ Skills Observe Notice your body sensations (coming through your eyes, ears, nose, skin, and tongue). ВүВү Pay attention on purpose, to the present moment. ВүВү Control your attention, but not w...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 4 (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 4вҖ“4b; pp. 78вҖ“82, 84вҖ“87) В« Taking Hold of Your Mind: вҖңWhatвҖқ Skills Observe Notice your body sensations (coming through your eyes, ears, nose, skin, and tongue). ВүВү Pay attention on purpose, to the present moment. ВүВү Control your attention, but not what you see. Push away nothing. Cling to nothing. ВүВү Practice wordless watching: Watch thoughts come into your mind and let them slip right by ВүВү like clouds in the sky. Notice each feeling, rising and falling, like waves in the ocean. Observe both inside and outside yourself. ВүВү Describe Put words on the experience. When a feeling or thought arises, or you do something, ВүВү acknowledge it. For example, say in your mind, вҖңSadness has just enveloped me,вҖқ or вҖңStomach muscles tightening,вҖң or вҖңA thought вҖҳI canвҖҷt do thisвҖҷ has come into my mind.вҖқ Label what you observe. Put a name on your feelings. Label a thought as just a thought, a ВүВү feeling as just a feeling, an action as just an action. Unglue your interpretations and opinions from the facts. Describe the вҖңwho, what, when, ВүВү and whereвҖқ that you observe. Just the facts. Remember, If you canвҖҷt observe it through your senses, you canвҖҷt describe it. ВүВү Participate Throw yourself completely into activities of the current moment. Do not separate yourself ВүВү from what is going on in the moment (dancing, cleaning, talking to a friend, feeling happy or feeling sad). Become one with whatever you are doing, completely forgetting yourself. Throw your ВүВү attention to the moment. Act intuitively from Wise Mind. Do just what is needed in each situationвҖ”a skillful dancer on ВүВү the dance floor, one with the music and your partner, neither willful nor sitting on your hands. Go with the flow. Respond with spontaneity. ВүВү From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Ideas for Practicing Observing
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 4a (p. 1 of 4) (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 4вҖ“4b; pp. 78вҖ“82, 84вҖ“87) Ideas for Practicing Observing By Coming Back to Your Senses Remember: Observing is bringing your mind back to the sensations of your body and mind. Observe with your eyes: 1. пҒұ Lie on the ground and watch the c...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 4a (p. 1 of 4) (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 4вҖ“4b; pp. 78вҖ“82, 84вҖ“87) Ideas for Practicing Observing By Coming Back to Your Senses Remember: Observing is bringing your mind back to the sensations of your body and mind. Observe with your eyes: 1. пҒұ Lie on the ground and watch the clouds in the sky. 2. пҒұ Walking slowly, stopping somewhere with a view, notice flowers, trees, and nature itself. 3. пҒұ Sit outside. Watch who and what go by in front of you, without following them with your head or your eyes. 4. пҒұ Notice the facial expression and movements of another person. Refrain from labeling the personвҖҷs emotions, thoughts, or interests. 5. пҒұ Notice just the eyes, lips, or hands of another person (or just one feature of an animal). 6. пҒұ Pick up a leaf, a flower, or a pebble. Look at it closely, trying to see each detail. 7. пҒұ Find something beautiful to look at, and spend a few minutes contemplating it. 8. Other: Observe sounds: 9. пҒұ Stop for a moment and just listen. Listen to the texture and shape of the sounds around you. Listen to the silences between the sounds. 10. пҒұ If someone is talking, listen to the pitch of the voice, to the smoothness or roughness of the sounds, to the clarity or the mumbling of the speech, to the pauses between the words. 11. пҒұ Listen to music, observing each note as it comes and the spaces between the notes. Try breathing the sounds into your body and letting them flow out again on your out breath. 12. Other: Observe smells around you: 13. пҒұ Breathing in, notice any smells around you. Bring something close to your nose, and notice the smells. Take it away, and then notice the smells again. Do they linger? 14. пҒұ When eating, notice the aroma of the food; when cooking, notice the aroma of the spices or other ingredients; when bathing, smell the soap or shampoo; when walking outside, notice the aroma of the air; when near flowers, bend down and вҖңsmell the roses.вҖқ 15. Other: Observe taste and the act of eating: 16. пҒұ Putting something in your mouth, pay attention to the taste. Keep it in your mouth, and notice all the taste sensations. 17. пҒұ Lick a lollipop or something else. Notice just the sensation of taste. 18. пҒұ Eat a meal, or even a part of a meal, paying attention to the taste of each mouthful. 19. Other: Observe urges to do something: When you are feeling an urge to do something impulsive, 20. пҒұ вҖңUrge-surfвҖқ by imagining that your urges are a surfboard and you are standing on the board, riding the waves. 21. пҒұ Notice any urge to avoid someone or something. 22. пҒұ Scan your entire body, and notice the sensations. Where in the body is the urge? 23. пҒұ When you are chewing your food, notice when you have the urge to swallow. 24. Other: (continued on next page) From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Observe sensations of touch on your skin:
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 4a (p. 2 of 4) Observe sensations of touch on your skin: 25. пҒұ Stroke your upper lip with your fingernail. вҖўвҖў Stop stroking, and notice how long it takes before you canвҖҷt sense your upper lip at all. 26. пҒұ When walking, notice the sensations of walkingвҖ”your feet hitting the groun...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 4a (p. 2 of 4) Observe sensations of touch on your skin: 25. пҒұ Stroke your upper lip with your fingernail. вҖўвҖў Stop stroking, and notice how long it takes before you canвҖҷt sense your upper lip at all. 26. пҒұ When walking, notice the sensations of walkingвҖ”your feet hitting the ground and rising up and down. Sometimes walk very slowly and notice. Sometimes walk very fast and notice. 27. пҒұ When sitting, notice your thighs on the chair. Notice the curve of your knees and your back. 28. пҒұ Pay attention to anything touching you. вҖўвҖў Try to feel your feet in your shoes, your body touching your clothes. вҖўвҖў Feel your arms touching a chair. вҖўвҖў Notice the sensations of your hands. 29. пҒұ Touch somethingвҖ”the wall, a fabric, a table top, a pet, a piece of fruit, a person. вҖўвҖў Notice the texture of what you feel, notice the sensations on your skin. вҖўвҖў Try it again with another part of your body. вҖўвҖў Notice the sensations again. 30. пҒұ Focus your attention on the sensations in your chest, your stomach, or your shoulders. 31. пҒұ Focus your attention on the place in your body where you feel tight or tense. 32. пҒұ Focus your attention on the space between your eyes. 33. Other: Observe your breath: Breathe evenly and gently, focusing your attention on: 34. пҒұ The movement of your stomach. вҖўвҖў As you begin to breathe in, allow your belly to rise in order to bring air into the lower half of your lungs. вҖўвҖў As the upper halves of your lungs begin to fill with air, your chest begins to rise. вҖўвҖў As you breathe out, notice your belly, then notice your chest. DonвҖҷt tire yourself. 35. пҒұ The pauses in your breathing. вҖўвҖў As you breathe in, notice the brief pause when your lungs have filled with air. вҖўвҖў As you breathe out, notice the brief pause when you have expelled all the air. 36. пҒұ The sensations in your nose as you breathe in and as you breathe out. вҖўвҖў As you breathe, close your mouth and breathe in through your nose, noticing the sensations traveling up and down your nostrils. 37. пҒұ Your breath while walking slowly. Breathe normally. вҖўвҖў Determine the length of your breathвҖ”the exhalation and the inhalationвҖ”by the number of your footsteps. Continue for a few minutes. вҖўвҖў Begin to lengthen your exhalation by one step. Do not force a longer inhalation. Let it be natural. вҖўвҖў Watch your inhalation carefully to see whether there is a desire to lengthen it. Now lengthen the exhalation by one more footstep. вҖўвҖў Watch to see whether the inhalation also lengthens by one step or not. вҖўвҖў Only lengthen the inhalation when you feel that it will be comfortable. вҖўвҖў After 20 breaths, return your breath to normal. 38. пҒұ Your breath while listening to a piece of music. вҖўвҖў Breathe long, light, and even breaths. вҖўвҖў Follow your breath; be master of it, while remaining aware of the movement and sentiments of the music. вҖўвҖў Do not get lost in the music, but continue to be master of your breath and yourself. 39. пҒұ Your breath while listening to a friendвҖҷs words and your own replies. Continue as with music. 40. Other: (continued on next page)

chunks.heading: Observe thoughts coming in and out of your mind:
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 4a (p. 3 of 4) Observe thoughts coming in and out of your mind: 41. пҒұ Notice thoughts as they come into your mind. вҖўвҖў Ask, вҖңWhere do thoughts come from?вҖқ вҖўвҖў Then watch them to see if you can see where they come into your mind. 42. пҒұ As you notice thoughts in your mind, notice the...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 4a (p. 3 of 4) Observe thoughts coming in and out of your mind: 41. пҒұ Notice thoughts as they come into your mind. вҖўвҖў Ask, вҖңWhere do thoughts come from?вҖқ вҖўвҖў Then watch them to see if you can see where they come into your mind. 42. пҒұ As you notice thoughts in your mind, notice the pauses between each thought. 43. пҒұ Imagine that your mind is the sky and that thoughts are clouds. вҖўвҖў Notice each thought-cloud as it drifts by, letting it drift in and out of your mind. вҖўвҖў Imagine thoughts as leaves on water flowing down a stream, as boats drifting by on the lake, or as train cars rolling by you. 44. пҒұ When worries go round and round in your mind, move your attention to the sensations in your body (those most intense right now). Then, keeping your attention on your body sensations, notice how long it takes for the worries to ooze away. 45. пҒұ Step back from your mind, as if you are on top of a mountain and your mind is just a boulder down below. вҖўвҖў Gaze at your mind, watching what thoughts come up when you are watching it. вҖўвҖў Come back into your mind before you stop. 46. пҒұ Watch for the first two thoughts that come into your mind. 47. Other: Imagine that your mind is a: 48. пҒұ Conveyor belt, and that thoughts and feelings are coming down the belt. вҖўвҖў Put each thought or feeling in a box, and then put it on the conveyor belt and let it go by. 49. пҒұ Conveyor belt, and that you are sorting thoughts and feelings as they come down the belt. вҖўвҖў Label the types of thoughts or feelings coming by (e.g., worry thoughts, thoughts about my past, thoughts about my mother, planning-what-to-do thoughts, angry feeling, sad feelings). вҖўвҖў Put them in boxes nearby for another time. 50. пҒұ River, and that thoughts and feelings are boats going down the river. вҖўвҖў Imagine sitting on the grass, watching the boats go by. вҖўвҖў Describe or label each boat as it goes by. вҖўвҖў Try not to jump on the boat. 51. пҒұ Railroad track, and that thoughts and feelings are train cars going by. вҖўвҖў Describe or label each as it goes by. Try not to jump on the train. 52. Other: Observe by expanding awareness: 53. пҒұ Breathing in, notice your breath. Then, keeping your breath in your awareness, on the next breath notice your hands. Then, keeping both in your awareness, on the next breath expand your awareness to sounds. вҖўвҖў Continue holding all three in awareness at the same time. вҖўвҖў Practice this awareness of threes at other times, selecting other things to be aware of. 54. пҒұ Keeping your focus on what you are currently doing, gently expand your awareness to include the space around you. 55. пҒұ Go hug a tree, and feel the sensations of the embrace. вҖўвҖў Attend to the embrace of the sheets and blankets or comforters around you as you lie in bed. вҖўвҖў Do this when you feel lonely and want to be loved or to love. 56. Other: (continued on next page)

chunks.heading: Open your mind to your senses:
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 4a (p. 4 of 4) Open your mind to your senses: 57. пҒұ Practice walking with your senses as wide open as you can make them. вҖўвҖў Notice what you hear, see, and feel. вҖўвҖў Notice what you feel when shifting your weight between each step. вҖўвҖў Notice your body experience as you turn. 58. пҒұ...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 4a (p. 4 of 4) Open your mind to your senses: 57. пҒұ Practice walking with your senses as wide open as you can make them. вҖўвҖў Notice what you hear, see, and feel. вҖўвҖў Notice what you feel when shifting your weight between each step. вҖўвҖў Notice your body experience as you turn. 58. пҒұ For one mouthful in a meal, pause with a spoonful or forkful of food. вҖўвҖў Look at what you are going to eat, smell it, and listen to it. Then, when you are ready, put it in your mouth. вҖўвҖў Note the taste, texture, temperature, and even the sound your teeth make in chewing your mouthful slowly. вҖўвҖў Note the changes in its taste, texture, temperature, and sound as you chew it to completion. Focus your mind on paying attention to each sensation that comes into your mind. 59. пҒұ вҖўвҖў Attend to sensations of sight, smell, touch, hearing, and taste, or to the thoughts generated by your brain. вҖўвҖў Notice sensations as they arise, and notice them as they fall away. вҖўвҖў Let your mind focus on each sensation as it arises. вҖўвҖў Notice each sensation with curiosity, allowing it to be. Examine the uniqueness of each sensation. 60. пҒұ Be here. Be in the present now. вҖўвҖў Take a moment to notice every sense you are aware of. вҖўвҖў To yourself, make a statement, about each sense: вҖңI feel the chair; the chair feels me.вҖқ вҖңI hear the heater; the heater hears me.вҖқ вҖңI see the wall; the wall sees me.вҖқ вҖңI hear a stomach growl; it hears me.вҖқ 61. пҒұ When a feeling arises within you, notice itвҖ”saying, for example, вҖңA feeling of sadness is arising within me.вҖқ 62. пҒұ When a thought arises within you, notice itвҖ”saying, for example, вҖңThe thought вҖҳIt is hot in hereвҖҷ is arising within me.вҖқ Take just a moment of your time, and practice вҖңnothing-to-doвҖқ mind. 63. пҒұ вҖўвҖў Let yourself become completely aware of your present experience, noticing sensations and the space around you. 64. пҒұ Find a small object, one you can hold in your hand. Place it in front of you on a table or in your lap. Observe it closelyвҖ”first not moving it, and then picking it up and turning it over and around, gazing at it from different angles and in different lights. Just notice shapes, colors, sizes, and other characteristics that are visible. вҖўвҖў Then change your focus to your fingers and hands touching the object. Notice the sensations of touching the object; notice the texture, temperature, and feel of the object. вҖўвҖў Put the object down. Close your eyes, and inhale and exhale deeply and slowly. вҖўвҖў Then, with beginnerвҖҷs mind, open your eyes. With new vision, once again notice the object. With beginnerвҖҷs mind, open to feeling new textures and sensations, explore the object with your fingers and hands. вҖўвҖў Put down the object, and once again focus your mind on inhaling and exhaling once. 65. Other:

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 4b
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 4b (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 4вҖ“4b; pp. 78вҖ“82, 84вҖ“87) Ideas for Practicing Describing Practice describing what you see outside of yourself: 1. пҒұ Lie on the ground and watch the clouds in the sky. Find and describe cloud patterns that you see. 2. пҒұ Sit on a bench on a busy stre...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 4b (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 4вҖ“4b; pp. 78вҖ“82, 84вҖ“87) Ideas for Practicing Describing Practice describing what you see outside of yourself: 1. пҒұ Lie on the ground and watch the clouds in the sky. Find and describe cloud patterns that you see. 2. пҒұ Sit on a bench on a busy street or at a park. Describe one thing about each person who walks by you. 3. пҒұ Find things in natureвҖ”a leaf, a drop of water, a pet or other animal. Describe each thing in as much detail as you can. 4. пҒұ Describe as accurately as you can what a person has just said to you. Check to see if you are correct. 5. пҒұ Describe a personвҖҷs face when the person seems angry, afraid, or sad. Notice and describe the shape, movement, and placement of the forehead, eyebrows, and eyes; the lips and mouth; the cheeks; and so on. 6. пҒұ Describe what a person has done or is doing now. Be very specific. Avoid describing intentions or outcomes of the behavior that you do not directly observe. Avoid judgmental language. 7. Other: Practice describing thoughts and feelings: 8. пҒұ Describe your feelings as they arise within you: вҖңA feeling of anger is arising within me.вҖқ 9. пҒұ Describe your thoughts when you feel a strong emotion: вҖңI feel X, and my thoughts are Y.вҖқ 10. пҒұ Describe your feelings after someone else does or says something: вҖңWhen you do X, I feel Y.вҖқ 11. пҒұ Describe thoughts, feelings, and what you observed others do: вҖңWhen you do X, I feel Y, and my thoughts are Z.вҖқ вҖңWhen X occurs, I feel Y, and my thoughts are Z.вҖқ 12. пҒұ Describe as many of your thoughts as you can while feeling a strong emotion. 13. Other: Practice describing your breathing: 14. пҒұ Each time you inhale and exhale, as you inhale, be aware that вҖңI am inhaling, 1.вҖқ When you exhale, be aware that вҖңI am exhaling, 1.вҖқ Remember to breathe from the stomach. When beginning the second inhalation, be aware that вҖңI am inhaling, 2.вҖқ And, slowly exhaling, be aware that вҖңI am exhaling, 2.вҖқ Continue on up through 10. After you have reached 10, return to 1. Whenever you lose count, return to 1. 15. пҒұ Begin to inhale gently and normally (from the stomach), describing in your mind that вҖңI am inhaling normally.вҖқ Exhale in awareness, вҖңI am exhaling normally.вҖқ Continue for three breaths. On the fourth breath, extend the inhalation, describing in your mind that вҖңI am breathing in a long inhalation.вҖқ Exhale in awareness, вҖңI am breathing out a long exhalation.вҖқ Continue for three breaths. 16. пҒұ Follow the entrance and exit of air. Say to yourself, вҖңI am inhaling and following the inhalation from its beginning to its end. I am exhaling and following the exhalation from its beginning to its end.вҖқ 17. Other:

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 4c
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 4c (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 4вҖ“4b; pp. 78вҖ“82, 84вҖ“87) Ideas for Practicing Participating Participate with awareness of connection to the universe: 1. пҒұ Focus your attention on where your body touches an object (floor or ground, air molecules, a chair or armrest, your bed sheet...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 4c (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 4вҖ“4b; pp. 78вҖ“82, 84вҖ“87) Ideas for Practicing Participating Participate with awareness of connection to the universe: 1. пҒұ Focus your attention on where your body touches an object (floor or ground, air molecules, a chair or armrest, your bed sheets and covers, your clothes, etc.). Try to see all the ways you are connected to and accepted by that object. Consider the function of that object with relation to you. That is, consider what the object does for you. Consider its kindness in doing that. Experience the sensation of touching the object, and focus your entire attention on that kindness until a sense of being connected or loved or cared for arises in your heart. Examples: Focus your attention on your feet touching the ground. Consider the kindness of the ground holding you up, providing a path for you to get to other things, not letting you fall away from everything else. Focus your attention on your body touching the chair you sit in. Consider how the chair accepts you totally, holds you up, supports your back, and keeps you from falling down on the floor. Focus your attention on the sheets and covers on your bed. Consider the touch of the sheets and covers holding you, surrounding and keeping you warm and comfortable. Consider the walls in the room. They keep out the wind and the cold and the rain. Think of how the walls are connected to you via the floor and the air in the room. Experience your connection to the walls that provide you with a secure place to do things. Go hug a tree. Think of how you and the tree are connected. Life is in you and in the tree and both of you are warmed by the sun, held by the air and supported by the earth. Try and experience the tree loving you by providing something to lean on, or by shading you. 2. пҒұ Dance to music. 3. пҒұ Sing along with music you are listening to. 4. пҒұ Sing in the shower. 5. пҒұ Sing and dance while watching TV. 6. пҒұ Jump out of bed and dance, or sing before getting dressed. 7. пҒұ Go to a church that sings, and join in the singing. 8. пҒұ Play karaoke with friends or at a karaoke club or bar. Throw yourself into what another person is saying. 9. пҒұ 10. пҒұ Go running, focusing only on running. 11. пҒұ Play a sport and throw yourself into playing. Become the count of the breath, becoming only вҖңoneвҖқ when you count 1, becoming only 12. пҒұ вҖңtwoвҖқ when you count 2, and so on. 13. пҒұ Become a word as you slowly say the word over and over and over. 14. пҒұ Take a class in improvisational acting. 15. пҒұ Take a dance class. 16. Other: From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 5
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 5 (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 5вҖ“5c; pp. 79вҖ“82, 88вҖ“93) В« Taking Hold of Your Mind: вҖңHowвҖқ Skills Nonjudgmentally See, but donвҖҷt evaluate as good or bad. Just the facts. ВүВү Accept each moment like a blanket spread out on the lawn, accepting both the rain and the ВүВү sun and each le...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 5 (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 5вҖ“5c; pp. 79вҖ“82, 88вҖ“93) В« Taking Hold of Your Mind: вҖңHowвҖқ Skills Nonjudgmentally See, but donвҖҷt evaluate as good or bad. Just the facts. ВүВү Accept each moment like a blanket spread out on the lawn, accepting both the rain and the ВүВү sun and each leaf that falls upon it. Acknowledge the difference between the helpful and the harmful, the safe and the dangerous, ВүВү but donвҖҷt judge them. Acknowledge your values, your wishes, your emotional reactions, but donвҖҷt judge them. ВүВү When you find yourself judging, donвҖҷt judge your judging. ВүВү One-ВӯMindfully Rivet yourself to now. Be completely present to this one moment. ВүВү Do one thing at a time. Notice the desire to be half-Вӯpresent, to be somewhere else, to go ВүВү somewhere else in your mind, to do something else, to multitaskвҖ”and then come back to one thing at a time. вҖўвҖў When you are eating, eat. вҖўвҖў When you are walking, walk. вҖўвҖў When you are worrying, worry. вҖўвҖў When you are planning, plan. вҖўвҖў When you are remembering, remember. Let go of distractions. If other actions, or other thoughts, or strong feelings distract you, go ВүВү back to what you are doingвҖ”again, and again, and again. Concentrate your mind. If you find you are doing two things at once, stopвҖ”go back to one ВүВү thing at a time (the opposite of multitasking!). Effectively Be mindful of your goals in the situation, and do what is necessary to achieve them. ВүВү Focus on what works. (DonвҖҷt let emotion mind get in the way of being effective.) ВүВү Play by the rules. ВүВү Act as skillfully as you can. Do what is needed for the situation you are inвҖ”not the situation ВүВү you wish you were in; not the one that is fair; not the one that is more comfortable. Let go of willfulness and sitting on your hands. ВүВү From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 5a
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 5a (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 5вҖ“5c; pp. 79вҖ“82, 88вҖ“93) Ideas for Practicing Nonjudgmentalness Leaving out comparisons, judgments, and assumptions: 1. пҒұ Practice observing judgmental thoughts and statements, saying in your mind, вҖңA judgmental thought arose in my mind.вҖқ 2. пҒұ Coun...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 5a (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 5вҖ“5c; pp. 79вҖ“82, 88вҖ“93) Ideas for Practicing Nonjudgmentalness Leaving out comparisons, judgments, and assumptions: 1. пҒұ Practice observing judgmental thoughts and statements, saying in your mind, вҖңA judgmental thought arose in my mind.вҖқ 2. пҒұ Count judgmental thoughts and statements (by moving objects or pieces of paper from one pocket to another, by clicking a sports counter, or by marking a piece of paper). 3. пҒұ Replace judgmental thoughts and statements with nonjudgmental thoughts and statements. Tips for replacing judgment by stating the facts: 1. Describe the facts of the event or situationвҖ”only what is observed with your senses. 2. Describe the consequences of the event. Keep to the facts. 3. Describe your own feelings in response to the facts (remember, emotions are not judgments). Observe your judgmental facial expressions, postures, and voice tones (including 4. пҒұ voice tones in your head). Change judgmental expressions, postures, and voice tones. 5. пҒұ 6. пҒұ Tell someone what you did today nonjudgmentally, or about an event that occurred. Stay very concrete; only relate what you observed directly. Write out a nonjudgmental description of an event that prompted an emotion. 7. пҒұ 8. пҒұ Write out a nonjudgmental blow-by-blow account of a particularly important episode in your day. Describe both what happened in your environment and what your thoughts, feelings, and actions were. Leave out any analysis of why something happened, or why you thought, felt, or acted as you did. Stick to the facts that you observed. 9. пҒұ Imagine a person you are angry with. Bring to mind what the person has done that has caused so much anger. Try to become that person, seeing life from that personвҖҷs point of view. Imagine that personвҖҷs feelings, thoughts, fears, hopes, and wishes. Imagine that personвҖҷs history and what has happened in his or her history. Imagine understanding that person. When judgmental, practice half-Вӯsmiling and/or willing hands. (See Distress Tolerance 10. пҒұ Handout 14: Half-ВӯSmiling and Willing Hands.) 11. Other: From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 5b
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 5b (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 5вҖ“5c; pp. 79вҖ“82, 88вҖ“93) Ideas for Practicing One-ВӯMindfulness Awareness while making tea or coffee. Prepare a pot of tea or coffee to serve a guest or 1. пҒұ to drink by yourself. Do each movement slowly, in awareness. Do not let one detail of your...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 5b (Mindfulness Worksheets 2вҖ“2c, 5вҖ“5c; pp. 79вҖ“82, 88вҖ“93) Ideas for Practicing One-ВӯMindfulness Awareness while making tea or coffee. Prepare a pot of tea or coffee to serve a guest or 1. пҒұ to drink by yourself. Do each movement slowly, in awareness. Do not let one detail of your movements go by without being aware of it. Know that your hand lifts the pot by its handle. Know that you are pouring the fragrant, warm tea or coffee into the cup. Follow each step in awareness. Breathe gently and more deeply than usual. Take hold of your breath if your mind strays. Awareness while washing the dishes. Wash the dishes consciously, as though each bowl 2. пҒұ is an object of contemplation. Consider each bowl sacred. Follow your breath to prevent your mind from straying. Do not try to hurry to get the job over with. Consider washing the dishes the most important thing in life. Awareness while hand-Вӯwashing clothes. Do not wash too many clothes at one time. 3. пҒұ Select only three or four articles of clothing. Find the most comfortable position to sit or stand, so as to prevent a backache. Scrub the clothes consciously. Hold your attention on every movement of your hands and arms. Pay attention to the soap and water. When you have finished scrubbing and rinsing, your mind and body will feel as clean and fresh as your clothes. Remember to maintain a half-smile and take hold of your breath whenever your mind wanders. Awareness while cleaning house. Divide your work into stages: straightening things and 4. пҒұ putting away books, scrubbing the toilet, scrubbing the bathroom, sweeping the floors, and dusting. Allow a good length of time for each task. Move slowly, three times more slowly than usual. Focus your attention fully on each task. For example, while placing a book on the shelf, look at the book; be aware of what book it is; know that you are in the process of placing it on the shelf; and know that you intend to put it in that specific place. Know that your hand reaches for the book and picks it up. Avoid any abrupt or harsh movement. Maintain awareness of the breath, especially when your thoughts wander. Awareness while taking a slow-Вӯmotion bath. Allow yourself 30вҖ“45 minutes to take a 5. пҒұ bath. DonвҖҷt hurry for even a second. From the moment you prepare the bath water to the moment you put on clean clothes, let every motion be light and slow. Be attentive of every movement. Place your attention on every part of your body, without discrimination or fear. Be aware of each stream of water on your body. By the time youвҖҷve finished, your mind will feel as peaceful and light as your body. Follow your breath. Think of yourself as being in a clean and fragrant lotus pond in the summer. Awareness with meditation. Sit comfortably on the floor with your back straight, on the 6. пҒұ floor or in a chair with both feet touching the floor. Close your eyes all the way, or open them slightly and gaze at something near. With each breath, say to yourself, quietly and gently, the word вҖңOne.вҖқ As you inhale, say the word вҖңOne.вҖқ As you exhale, say the word вҖңOne,вҖқ calmly and slowly. Try to collect your whole mind and put it into this one word. When your mind strays, return gently to saying вҖңOne.вҖқ If you start wanting to move, try not to move. Just gently observe wanting to move. Continue practicing a little past wanting to stop. Just gently observe wanting to stop. 7. Other: Note. Adapted from The Miracle of Mindfulness (pp. 84вҖ“87) by Thich Nhat Hanh. Copyright 1975, 1976 by Thich Nhat Hanh. Preface and English translation copyright 1975, 1976, 1987 by Mobi Ho. Adapted by permission of Beacon Press, Boston. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 7a
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 7a Wise Mind from a Spiritual Perspective Thoughts, attitudes, and actions designed to help us express or experience connection to: Wise Mind as . . . вҖўвҖў The sacred, the divine within, the transcendent. Contemplative practice вҖўвҖў God, the Great Spirit, the Absolute, Mindfulness El...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 7a Wise Mind from a Spiritual Perspective Thoughts, attitudes, and actions designed to help us express or experience connection to: Wise Mind as . . . вҖўвҖў The sacred, the divine within, the transcendent. Contemplative practice вҖўвҖў God, the Great Spirit, the Absolute, Mindfulness Elohim, the nameless one, Brahma, Allah, Meditation Parvardigar. Contemplative prayer вҖўвҖў Ultimate reality, the totality, the source, our Contemplative action essential nature, our true self, the core of Centering prayer our being, the ground of being. вҖўвҖў No self, emptiness. Experience where a deeper layer of reality rises to consciousness. A reality Wise Mind experience that has always been there but has been from a spiritual perspective misperceived. An experience of expansion of consciousness; the experience of unity and oneness within the sacred. 1. Direct experience: Experience without words of ultimate reality. 2. Experience of unity: Awareness of oneness and of no distance between oneself, reality, and all other beings. 3. Without words: Experience of reality that cannot be grasped and can only be Wise Mind described with metaphors and stories. from the perspective of mysticism 4. Certain: During the experience, certainty (seven characteristics of the experience is total, undeniable, of mystical experiences) clear. 5. Practical: Experience that is concretely beneficial to oneвҖҷs life and well-being. 6. Integrative: Experience that establishes harmony of love, compassion, mercy, kindness; quieting of extreme emotions. 7. Sapiential: Experience that leads to wisdom, enhances capacity for intuitive knowledge. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Mindfulness Handout 8
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 8 (Mindfulness Worksheet 6; p. 97) Practicing Loving Kindness to Increase Love and Compassion What Is Loving Kindness? Loving kindness is a mindfulness practice designed to increase love and compassion first for ourselves and then for our loved ones, for friends, for those we are...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 8 (Mindfulness Worksheet 6; p. 97) Practicing Loving Kindness to Increase Love and Compassion What Is Loving Kindness? Loving kindness is a mindfulness practice designed to increase love and compassion first for ourselves and then for our loved ones, for friends, for those we are angry with, for difficult people, for enemies, and then for all beings. Loving kindness can protect us from developing and holding on to judgmentalness, ill will, and hostile feelings toward ourselves and others. Practicing Loving Kindness Practicing loving kindness is like saying a prayer for yourself or someone else. As when you are asking or praying for something for yourself or others, you actively send loving and kind wishes, and recite in your mind words and phrases that express good will toward yourself and others. Loving Kindness Instructions 1. Choose a person to send loving kindness toward. Do not select a person you do not want to relate to with kindness and compassion. Start with yourself, or, if this is too difficult, with a person you already love. 2. Sitting, standing, or lying down, begin by breathing slowly and deeply. Opening the palms of your hands, gently bring the person to mind. 3. Radiate loving kindness by reciting a set of warm wishes, such as вҖңMay I be happy,вҖқ вҖңMay I be at peace,вҖқ вҖңMay I be healthy,вҖқ вҖңMay I be safe,вҖқ or another set of positive wishes of your own. Repeat the script slowly, and focus on the meaning of each word as you say it in your mind. (If you have distracting thoughts, just notice them as they come and go and gently bring your mind back to your script.) Continue until you feel yourself immersed in loving kindness. 4. Gradually work yourself up through loved ones, friends, those you are angry with, difficult people, enemies, and finally all beings. For example, use a script such as вҖңMay John be happy,вҖқ вҖңMay John be at peace,вҖқ and so on (or вҖңJohn, may you be happy,вҖқ вҖңMay you be at peace,вҖқ and so on), as you concentrate on radiating loving kindness to John. 5. Practice each day, starting with yourself and then moving to others. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Ideas for Practicing Balancing
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 9a (p. 1 of 2) (Mindfulness Worksheets 7, 8, 9; pp. 98вҖ“104) Ideas for Practicing Balancing Doing Mind and Being Mind The mindfulness skills require a lot of practice. The practice ideas below are to help you act skillfully in everyday life, bringing together doing activities of e...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 9a (p. 1 of 2) (Mindfulness Worksheets 7, 8, 9; pp. 98вҖ“104) Ideas for Practicing Balancing Doing Mind and Being Mind The mindfulness skills require a lot of practice. The practice ideas below are to help you act skillfully in everyday life, bringing together doing activities of everyday life with being mind. Wise Mind reading. To increase your desire for mindfulness, find readings or quotations 1. пҒұ that have the effect of making you actually want to practice mindfulness in your everyday life. Put these quotations at strategic spots in your life (e.g., near the coffee maker), and then while you are waiting for other things, read the inspirational messages. Wise Mind reminders. Set an alarm at home, at work, or (if possible) on your cell phone or 2. пҒұ watch to go off randomly or at set times. Use the alarm as a reminder to be mindful of your current activities. (See www.mindfulnessdc.org/bell/index.html or a similar Internet site for a free mindfulness clock to download onto your computer.) Set up automatic text messages or Twitter messages to remind yourself. Write out mindfulness quotations that you like, and tape them in strategic places where you will see them as reminders to practice mindfulness. 3. пҒұ Wise Mind in the routine of daily life. Choose one routine activity in your daily life (such as brushing your teeth, getting dressed, making coffee or tea, working on a task). Make a deliberate effort to bring moment-to-Вӯmoment awareness to that activity. 4. пҒұ вҖңJust this one momentвҖқ Wise Mind. When you begin to feel overwhelmed or frazzled, say, вҖңJust this one moment, just this one task,вҖқ to remind yourself that your only requirement at the moment is to do one thing in the momentвҖ”wash one dish, take one step, move one set of muscles. In this moment, let the next moment go until you get there. (continued on next page) Note. Exercises 3 and 4 are from Segal, Z. V., Williams, J. M. G., & Teasdale, J. D. (2013). Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for depression: A new approach to preventing relapse (2nd ed.). New York: Guilford Press. Copyright 2013 by The Guilford Press. Adapted by permission. All other exercises are adapted from Kabat-Zinn, J. (1990). Full catastrophe living: Using the wisdom of your body and mind to face stress, pain, and illness. New York: Delacorte Press. Copyright 1990 by Jon Kabat-Zinn. Adapted by permission of Random House. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Wise Mind awareness of events. Notice events in your everyday life (both pleasant and
chunks.preview: Mindfulness Handout 9a (p. 2 of 2) 5. пҒұ Wise Mind awareness of events. Notice events in your everyday life (both pleasant and unpleasant), even if they are only very small (such as warm water on your hands when washing, the taste of something you eat, the feel of wind on your face, the fact that you...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Handout 9a (p. 2 of 2) 5. пҒұ Wise Mind awareness of events. Notice events in your everyday life (both pleasant and unpleasant), even if they are only very small (such as warm water on your hands when washing, the taste of something you eat, the feel of wind on your face, the fact that your car is running out of gas or that you are tired). 6. пҒұ Wise Mind awareness of what needs to be done. When relaxing after a hard dayвҖҷs work or at a break during the day, stay aware of what needs to be done and focus on doing what is needed. 7. пҒұ Wise Mind willingness. Practice willingness to do what is needed when you are asked, or when you see that something needs to be done. Do what is needed with a balance of being and doing, focusing the mind, immersing yourself in the task. 8. пҒұ Three-minute WISE MIND: Slowing down вҖңdoing mindвҖқ in your everyday life вҖўвҖў Bring yourself into the present moment by adopting a вҖңwide-awakeвҖқ posture, and then, in Wise Mind, ask, вҖңWhat is my experience right now? What thoughts and images are going through my mind?вҖқ Notice them as mental events, as neural firing in your brain. Next ask, вҖңWhat are my feelings and sensations in my body?вҖқ Notice these as they come into your awareness. Then say, вҖңOK, this is how it is right now.вҖқ вҖўвҖў Settle into Wise Mind and focus your entire attention on your breath as it goes in and as it goes out, one breath after another. Gather yourself all together, and focus on the movements of your chest and abdomen, the rise and fall of your breath, moment by moment, breath by breath as best you can. Let your breath become an anchor to bring you into the present moment. вҖўвҖў Once you have gathered yourself to some extent, allow your awareness to expand. As well as being aware of the breath, include also a sense of the body as a whole, your posture, your facial expression, your hands. Follow the breath as if your whole body is breathing. When you are ready, step back into your activities, acting from Wise Mind of your whole body in the present moment. Other Wise Mind practice ideas: 9. пҒұ

Make another list of the pros and cons of not practicing mindfulness skills. Check the facts to be...
chunks.text: Mindfulness Worksheet 1 (Mindfulness Handouts 1, 7; pp. 45, 68) Pros and Cons of Practicing Mindfulness Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Make a list of the pros and cons of practicing mindfulness skills. Make another list of the pros and cons of not practicing mindfulness skills. Check the facts to be sure that you are correct in your assessment of advantages and disadvantages. Rate Willingness to Practice (0 = None; 100 = Very High) Before: After: Fill this worksheet out when you are: вҖўвҖў Trying to decide whether to work on becoming more mindful of the moments in your life. вҖўвҖў Feeling willful; saying no to letting go of emotion mind or extreme reasonable mind. вҖўвҖў Resisting observing the present moment, rather than escaping it or trying to control it. вҖўвҖў Resisting giving up your interpretations of others or yourself, rather than just describing. вҖўвҖў Resisting throwing yourself into the flow of the moment; wanting to stand on the outside. вҖўвҖў Feeling threatened whenever you think of letting go of judgments. вҖўвҖў Not in the mood for being effective instead of proving you are right. When you are filling out this worksheet, think about these questions: вҖўвҖў Is a mindless life in your best interest (i.e., effective), or not in your best interest (i.e., ineffective)? вҖўвҖў Will refusing to go into Wise Mind solve a problem, or make a new problem for you? вҖўвҖў Is observing the moment without reacting to it immediately likely to increase your freedom, or decrease it? вҖўвҖў Is being attached to your thoughts instead of the facts you can describe useful, or not? вҖўвҖў Is staying judgmental helping you change the things you want to change, or getting in the way? вҖўвҖў Is it more important to be effective, or to be right? Stay Mindless, Judgmental, Ineffective Practice Mindfulness Pros Stay Mindless, Judgmental, Ineffective Practice Mindfulness Cons What did you decide to do? Is this the best decision (in Wise Mind)? List any and all wise things you did this week. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. chunks.text: Mindfulness Worksheet 4a (Mindfulness Handouts 4вҖ“4c; pp. 53вҖ“59) Observing, Describing, Participating Checklist Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Check off mindfulness skills that you use when you use them. You can check each skill up to four times. If you practice a skill more than four times, extend your checks toward the edge of the page, or use the back of the page if needed. Practice observing: Check off an exercise each time you do one. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 1. What you see: Watch without following what you see. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 2. Sounds: Sounds around you, pitch and sound of someoneвҖҷs voice, music. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 3. Smells around you: Aroma of food, soap, air as you walk. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 4. The taste of what you eat and the act of eating. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 5. Urges to do something: Urge-surf, notice urge to avoid, notice where in body urge is. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 6. Body sensations: Body scan, sensation of walking, body touching something. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 7. Thoughts coming in and out of your mind: Imagine your mind as a river, as a conveyor belt. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 8. Your breath: Movement of stomach, sensations of air in and out nose. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 9. By expanding awareness: To your entire body, to space around you, to hugging a tree. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 10. By opening the mind: To each sensation arising, not attaching, letting go of each. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 11. Other (describe): Practice describing: Check off an exercise each time you do one. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 12. What you see outside of your body. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 13. Thoughts, feelings, and body sensations inside yourself. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 14. Your breathing. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 15. Other (describe): Practice participating: Check off an exercise each time you do one. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 16. Dance to music. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 17. Sing along with music you are listening to. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 18. Sing in the shower. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 19. Sing and dance while watching TV. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 20. Jump out of bed and dance or sing before getting dressed. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 21. Go to a church that sings and join in the singing. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 22. Play karaoke with friends or at a karaoke club or bar. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 23. Throw yourself into what another person is saying. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 24. Go running, riding, skating, walking; become one with the activity. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 25. Play a sport and throw yourself into playing. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 26. Become the count of your breath, becoming only вҖңoneвҖқ when you count 1, becoming only вҖңtwoвҖқ when you count 2, and so on. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 27. Become a word as you slowly say the word over and over and over. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 28. Throw caution to the wind, and throw yourself into a social or work activity. пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ пҒұ 29. chunks.text: Mindfulness Worksheet 10a (Mindfulness Handout 10; p. 74) Analyzing Yourself on the Middle Path Due Date: Name: Week Starting: 1. Figure out where you are off the middle path, toward one extreme or the other. For each of the following Wise Mind dilemmas, put an X on the line that represents where you think you are most of the time. If you are fairly balanced, put the X in the middle. If you are out of balance, put the X near the end that you are too extreme on. Reasonable mind Emotion mind Doing mind Nothing-to-do mind Intense desire for Radical acceptance of change of the moment what is Self-Вӯdenial Self-Вӯindulgence 2. Choose one dilemma. Describe very specifically what you are doing that is too much, and then describe what you do too little of. Too little ___________________________ Too much ___________________________ ___________________________ ___________________________ ___________________________ ___________________________ 3. Check the facts. Check for interpretations and opinions. Make sure that your list of activities you do too much of or too little of is in fact accurate. Check your own values in Wise Mind: Be sure to work on your middle path, not someone elseвҖҷs. Also check for judgments. Avoid вҖңgood,вҖқ вҖңbad,вҖқ and judgmental language. Rewrite any items above if needed so that they are factual and nonjudgmental. 4. Decide on one (or at most two) very specific things to do in the next week to get closer to balance. Do less Do more ___________________________ ___________________________ ___________________________ ___________________________ ___________________________ ___________________________ 5. Describe what you did since last week: 6. Rate how effective the practice was in helping you become more balanced on the middle path.
