chunks.heading: Interpersonal
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets Interpersonal effectiveness skills help you build new relationships, strengthen current ones, and deal with conflict situations. They help you effectively ask for what you want and say no to unwanted requests. After a few han...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets Interpersonal effectiveness skills help you build new relationships, strengthen current ones, and deal with conflict situations. They help you effectively ask for what you want and say no to unwanted requests. After a few handouts and worksheets for Goals and Factors That Interfere, three main groups of forms for interpersonal effectiveness skills are provided in DBT. The first set focuses on Obtaining Objectives SkillfullyвҖ”that is, how to get what you want from others, while also maintaining your relationships and your self-Вӯrespect. The second set, Building Relationships and Ending Destructive Ones, focuses on how to find friends, get them to like you, and maintain the relationships, as well as on how to end damaging relationships. The third set covers Walking the Middle Path skills, which in this module have to do with balancing acceptance and change in relationships. Goals and Factors That Interfere вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 1: Goals of Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills. This first handout overviews the goals for each of the three main sections of this module. The major overall goal is to be effective in getting what you want skillfully. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 1: Pros and Cons of Using Interpersonal Skills. Use this worksheet to decide whether to use interpersonal skills instead of power tactics or giving up and giving in to another person. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2: Factors in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness. Lack of skills is only one factor that may prevent you from being effective with other people. This handout is helpful not only early in the module, but later in troubleshooting difficulties with using interpersonal effectiveness skills.

chunks.heading: 110 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills
chunks.preview: 110 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills It can then be used with Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 7: Troubleshooting Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills, and Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 9: Troubleshooting: When What You Are Doing IsnвҖҷt Working. These two worksheets cover the same topi...
chunks.text: 110 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills It can then be used with Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 7: Troubleshooting Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills, and Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 9: Troubleshooting: When What You Are Doing IsnвҖҷt Working. These two worksheets cover the same topics, organized in the same sequence as Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2a: Myths in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness. This handout can be useful if thoughts and beliefs get in the way of using interpersonal skills effectively. Use it with Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 2: Challenging Myths in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness. Obtaining Objectives Skillfully вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 3: Overview: Obtaining Objectives Skillfully. This handout overviews the skills covered in this section. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 4: Clarifying Goals in Interpersonal Situations. Clarifying your goals is the first and most important interpersonal skill. It is the essential task of figuring out (1) what you actually want in any given situation and how important that is, compared to (2) keeping a positive relationship and (3) keeping your own self-Вӯrespect. The skills you use depend on the relative importance of these three goals. Use this handout with Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 3: Figuring Out Goals in Interpersonal Situations. In describing the вҖңPrompting EventвҖқ on this worksheet, remember to use the mindfulness вҖңwhatвҖқ skill of describing. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5: Guidelines for Objectives Effectiveness: Getting What You Want (DEAR MAN). This handout describes the skills for asking for something, saying no to anotherвҖҷs request, and resisting pressure and maintaining your point of view. The skills are Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce; and (stay) Mindful, Appear confident, and Negotiate. You can use the term DEAR MAN to remember these. Two different worksheets can be used with this handout, as described next. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 4: Writing Out Interpersonal Effectiveness Scripts. This worksheet is useful for figuring out what to say and do before practicing DEAR MAN skills. Notice also that the worksheet requires you to first write down your objectives, relationship, and self-Вӯrespect goals. This worksheet can also be used for GIVE and FAST skills (see below). вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 5: Tracking Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Use. This worksheet can be used to track your use of interpersonal skills. It asks you to figure out and write down your priorities and asks about conflicts in priorities. Finally, it asks you to record whether or not your objective was met, and what effect the interaction had on the relationship and your self-Вӯrespect. This worksheet can be used with DEAR MAN, GIVE, and FAST skills. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5a: Applying DEAR MAN Skills to a

chunks.heading: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 111
chunks.preview: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 111 Difficult Current Interaction. This handout gives examples of how to handle situations where the other person also has very good interpersonal skills and refuses legitimate requests or keeps asking despite being told no. Use Interpersonal Effectiveness W...
chunks.text: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 111 Difficult Current Interaction. This handout gives examples of how to handle situations where the other person also has very good interpersonal skills and refuses legitimate requests or keeps asking despite being told no. Use Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 4, 5, or both with this handout (see above). вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6: Guidelines for Relationship Effectiveness: Keeping the Relationship (GIVE). Relationship effectiveness skills are aimed at maintaining or improving your relationship with the other person while you try to get what you want in the interaction. The term GIVE is a way to remember these skills. It stands for (be) Gentle, (act) Interested, Validate, and (use an) Easy manner. Use Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 4, 5, or both with this handout. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6a: Expanding the V in GIVE: Levels of Validation. This handout lists six different ways to validate. (See also Interpersonal Effectiveness Handouts 17 and 18, described later, for more on validation.) Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheets 4 and 5 can be used with this handout. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 7: Guidelines for Self-ВӯRespect Effectiveness: Keeping Respect for Yourself (FAST). Self-Вӯrespect effectiveness skills help you to keep or improve your self-Вӯrespect while you try to get what you want in an interaction. The term FAST is a way to remember these skills: (be) Fair, (no) Apologies, Stick to values, and (be) Truthful. Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheets 4 and 5 can be used with this handout. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8: Evaluating Options for Whether or How Intensely to Ask for Something or Say No. Before asking for something or saying no to another, consider how intensely to ask or say noвҖ”and whether to ask or say no at all. This handout lists the factors to consider in making a decision. Use Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 6: The Dime Game: Figuring Out How Strongly to Ask or Say No with this handout to figure out your best option in a particular situation. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 9: Troubleshooting: When What You Are Doing IsnвҖҷt Working. Difficulty in obtaining an objective can be due to many possible factors. When you can identify the problem, you can often solve it and be more effective in getting what you want. This handout provides questions for diagnosing which factors are reducing your interpersonal effectiveness. Use Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 7: Troubleshooting Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills with this handout. Building Relationships and Ending Destructive Ones вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 10: Overview: Building Relationships and Ending Destructive Ones. This handout briefly overviews the skills taught in this section of the module. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 11: Finding and Getting People to Like You. Finding potential friends and getting them to like you often both require an

chunks.heading: 112 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills
chunks.preview: 112 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills active effort. The handout summarizes where to look and how to look. Record your practice efforts for this on Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 8: Finding and Getting People to Like You. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 11a: Identifying Skills to Fi...
chunks.text: 112 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills active effort. The handout summarizes where to look and how to look. Record your practice efforts for this on Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 8: Finding and Getting People to Like You. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 11a: Identifying Skills to Find People and Get Them to Like You. This is a quick multiple-Вӯchoice quiz on the information in Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 11. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 12: Mindfulness of Others. Friendships are easier to form and last longer when we remember to be mindful of the other person. Notice that the three mindfulness skills described on this handout are the three core mindfulness вҖңwhatвҖқ skills (observing, describing, and participating) taught in the Mindfulness module. Use Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 9: Mindfulness of Others to record practice of this skill. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 12a: Identifying Mindfulness of Others. This is a brief multiple-Вӯchoice quiz on the skill of mindfulness of others. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 13: Ending Relationships. The skills for ending relationships described on this handout are drawn from the Mindfulness (Wise Mind), Emotion Regulation (problem solving, coping ahead, opposite action), and Interpersonal Effectiveness (DEAR MAN, GIVE FAST) skills modules. The one new skill is practicing safety first when ending abusive or life-Вӯthreatening relationships. If you are thinking about ending a relationship, use Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 10: Ending Relationships to weigh the factors and plan for use of these skills. If trying to leave an abusive or dangerous relationship, call a domestic violence hotline number first (either a local number or the national number listed on the worksheet). Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 1: Pros and Cons of Using Interpersonal Skills may also be useful with this handout. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 13a: Identifying How to End Relationships. This is a brief multiple-Вӯchoice quiz on how to end relationships. Walking the Middle Path вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 14: Overview: Walking the Middle Path. This handout briefly overviews the skills in this section: dialectics, validation, and behavior change strategies. These skills help you to effectively manage yourself and your relationships. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 15: Dialectics. A dialectical stance is essential for walking a middle path and for decreasing a sense of isolation, conflict, and polarities. This handout outlines the basics of a dialectical perspective. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16: How to Think and Act Dialectically. This is an extension of Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 15 and gives examples of how to think and act dialectically. There are three worksheets with different formats for recording dialectics practice, described next.

chunks.heading: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 113
chunks.preview: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 113 вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 11: Practicing Dialectics, Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 11a: Dialectics Checklist, and Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 11b: Noticing When YouвҖҷre Not Dialectical, can be used with Interpersonal Effec...
chunks.text: Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets вҖў 113 вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 11: Practicing Dialectics, Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 11a: Dialectics Checklist, and Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 11b: Noticing When YouвҖҷre Not Dialectical, can be used with Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16. Worksheet 11 provides space for recording two practices over the week. Worksheet 11a provides for multiple practices of multiple skills. Worksheet 11b is intended to help raise awareness of opportunities to be dialectical and of the consequences when not being dialectical. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16a: Examples of Opposite Sides That Can Both Be True. Dialectics tells us that the universe is filled with opposing sides, and that two things that seem like opposites can both be true. This handout lists examples of opposites that can both be true. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16b: Important Opposites to Balance. This handout lists opposite aspects of life and living that are important to keep in balance. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16c: Identifying Dialectics. This handout is a brief multiple-Вӯchoice quiz. It asks you to check the most dialectical responses. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 17: Validation. Validation of othersвҖҷ feelings, beliefs, experiences, and actions is essential in building any relationship of trust and intimacy. This handout reviews what validation is, what is most important to validate, and key points to remember about validation. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 18: A вҖңHow ToвҖқ Guide to Validation. This handout lists the six levels of validation and gives examples of each. Fill out Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 12: Validating Others whenever you have an opportunity to practice validation skills, whether or not you actually practiced the skills. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 18a: Identifying Validation. This handout is a brief multiple-Вӯchoice quiz on validation. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 19: Recovering from Invalidation. Invalidation can be helpful or harmful. Either way, it usually hurts. This handout lists how to respond effectively when you are invalidated by someone. Fill out Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 13: Self-ВӯValidation and Self-ВӯRespect whenever you have an opportunity to practice self-Вӯvalidation skills whether or not you actually practiced them. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 19a: Identifying Self-ВӯValidation. This is a brief multiple-Вӯchoice quiz on responding to invalidation. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 20: Strategies for Increasing the Probability of Desired Behaviors. This handout describes very effective strategies for increasing behaviors you want in yourself or others: behavior reinforcement and new behavior shaping. To be effective in changing behaviors, learn these strategies and put them into action. To record your practice, use Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 14: Changing Behavior with Reinforcement.

chunks.heading: 114 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills
chunks.preview: 114 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 21: Strategies for Decreasing or Stopping Undesired Behaviors. This handout describes effective strategies for decreasing or stopping unwanted behaviorsвҖ”Вӯextinction, satiating, and punishment. To record your practice, us...
chunks.text: 114 вҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 21: Strategies for Decreasing or Stopping Undesired Behaviors. This handout describes effective strategies for decreasing or stopping unwanted behaviorsвҖ”Вӯextinction, satiating, and punishment. To record your practice, use Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 15: Changing Behavior by Extinguishing or Punishing It. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 22: Tips for Using Behavior Change Strategies Effectively. Reinforcement, extinction, and punishment each involve different kinds of consequences. This handout outlines important issues in selecting and implementing consequences. вҖўвҖў Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 22a: Identifying Effective Behavior Change Strategies. This is a brief multiple-Вӯchoice quiz on behavior change strategies.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2 В«
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2 В« Factors in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness You DonвҖҷt Have the Interpersonal Skills You Need ВүВү You DonвҖҷt Know What You Want You have the skills, but canвҖҷt decide what you really want from the other person. ВүВү You canвҖҷt figure out how to balance your nee...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2 В« Factors in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness You DonвҖҷt Have the Interpersonal Skills You Need ВүВү You DonвҖҷt Know What You Want You have the skills, but canвҖҷt decide what you really want from the other person. ВүВү You canвҖҷt figure out how to balance your needs versus the other personвҖҷs needs: ВүВү ВүВүAsking for too much versus not asking for anything. ВүВүSaying no to everything versus giving in to everything. Your Emotions Are Getting in the Way You have the skills, but emotions (anger, pride, contempt, fear, shame, guilt) ВүВү control what you do. You Forget Your Long-Term Goals for Short-Term Goals You put your immediate urges and wants ahead of your long-term goals. The ВүВү future vanishes from your mind. Other People Are Getting in Your Way You have the skills but other people get in the way. ВүВү Other people are more powerful than you. ВүВү Other people may be threatened or may not like you if you get what you want. ВүВү Other people may not do what you want unless you sacrifice your self-Вӯrespect, at ВүВү least a little. Your Thoughts and Beliefs Are Getting in the Way Worries about negative consequences if you ask for what you want or say no to ВүВү someoneвҖҷs request get in the way of acting effectively. Beliefs that you donвҖҷt deserve what you want stop you in your tracks. ВүВү Beliefs that others donвҖҷt deserve what they want make you ineffective. ВүВү From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2a
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2a (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 2; p. 168) Myths in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness Myths in the Way of Objectives Effectiveness ВүВү1. I donвҖҷt deserve to get what I want or need. ВүВү2. If I make a request, this will show that I am a very weak person...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2a (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 2; p. 168) Myths in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness Myths in the Way of Objectives Effectiveness ВүВү1. I donвҖҷt deserve to get what I want or need. ВүВү2. If I make a request, this will show that I am a very weak person. ВүВү3. I have to know whether a person is going to say yes before I make a request. ВүВү4. If I ask for something or say no, I canвҖҷt stand it if someone gets upset with me. ВүВү5. If they say no, it will kill me. ВүВү6. Making requests is a really pushy (bad, self-Вӯcentered, selfish, etc.) thing to do. ВүВү7. Saying no to a request is always a selfish thing to do. ВүВү8. I should be willing to sacrifice my own needs for others. ВүВү9. I must be really inadequate if I canвҖҷt fix this myself. 10. Obviously, the problem is just in my head. If I would just think differently I wouldnвҖҷt have to ВүВү bother everybody else. 11. If I donвҖҷt have what I want or need, it doesnвҖҷt make any difference; I donвҖҷt care really. ВүВү 12. Skillfulness is a sign of weakness. ВүВү Other myth: Other myth: Myths in the Way of Relationship and Self-ВӯRespect Effectiveness 13. I shouldnвҖҷt have to ask (say no); they should know what I want (and do it). ВүВү 14. They should have known that their behavior would hurt my feelings; I shouldnвҖҷt have to tell ВүВү them. 15. I shouldnвҖҷt have to negotiate or work at getting what I want. ВүВү 16. Other people should be willing to do more for my needs. ВүВү 17. Other people should like, approve of, and support me. ВүВү 18. They donвҖҷt deserve my being skillful or treating them well. ВүВү 19. Getting what I want when I want it is most important. ВүВү 20. I shouldnвҖҷt be fair, kind, courteous, or respectful if others are not so toward me. ВүВү 21. Revenge will feel so good; it will be worth any negative consequences. ВүВү 22. Only wimps have values. ВүВү 23. Everybody lies. ВүВү 24. Getting what I want is more important than how I get it; the ends really do justify the means. ВүВү Other myth: Other myth: From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 4
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 4 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 3; p. 173) пӮ« Clarifying Goals in Interpersonal Situations Objectives Effectiveness: Getting What You Want from Another Person вҖўвҖў Obtaining your legitimate rights. вҖўвҖў Getting another person to do something you want that pers...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 4 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 3; p. 173) пӮ« Clarifying Goals in Interpersonal Situations Objectives Effectiveness: Getting What You Want from Another Person вҖўвҖў Obtaining your legitimate rights. вҖўвҖў Getting another person to do something you want that person to do. вҖўвҖў Saying no to an unwanted or unreasonable request. вҖўвҖў Resolving an interpersonal conflict. вҖўвҖў Getting your opinion or point of view taken seriously. Questions 1. What specific results or changes do I want from this interaction? 2. What do I have to do to get the results? What will work? Relationship Effectiveness: Keeping and Improving the Relationship вҖўвҖў Acting in such a way that the other person keeps liking and respecting you. вҖўвҖў Balancing immediate goals with the good of the long-term relationship. вҖўвҖў Maintaining relationships that matter to you. Questions 1. How do I want the other person to feel about me after the interaction is over (whether or not I get the results or changes I want)? 2. What do I have to do to get (or keep) this relationship? Self-ВӯRespect Effectiveness: Keeping or Improving Self-ВӯRespect вҖўвҖў Respecting your own values and beliefs. вҖўвҖў Acting in a way that makes you feel moral. вҖўвҖў Acting in a way that makes you feel capable and effective. Questions 1. How do I want to feel about myself after the interaction is over (whether or not I get the results or changes I want)? 2. What do I have to do to feel that way about myself? What will work? From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5 (p. 1 of 2)
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5 (p. 1 of 2) (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheets 4, 5; pp. 174вҖ“175) пӮ« Guidelines for Objectives Effectiveness: Getting What You Want (DEAR MAN) A way to remember these skills is to remember the term DEAR MAN: Describe Express Assert Reinforce (Stay) Mindful A...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5 (p. 1 of 2) (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheets 4, 5; pp. 174вҖ“175) пӮ« Guidelines for Objectives Effectiveness: Getting What You Want (DEAR MAN) A way to remember these skills is to remember the term DEAR MAN: Describe Express Assert Reinforce (Stay) Mindful Appear Confident Negotiate D escribe Describe the current situation (if necessary). Stick to the facts. Tell the person exactly what you are reacting to. вҖңYou told me you would be home by dinner but you didnвҖҷt get here until 11.вҖқ E xpress Express your feelings and opinions about the situation. DonвҖҷt assume that the other person knows how you feel. вҖңWhen you come home so late, I start worrying about you.вҖқ Use phrases such as вҖңI wantвҖқ instead of вҖңYou should,вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt wantвҖқ instead of вҖңYou shouldnвҖҷt.вҖқ A ssert Assert yourself by asking for what you want or saying no clearly. Do not assume that others will figure out what you want. Remember that others cannot read your mind. вҖңI would really like it if you would call me when you are going to be late.вҖқ R einforce Reinforce (reward) the person ahead of time (so to speak) by explaining positive effects of getting what you want or need. If necessary, also clarify the negative consequences of not getting what you want or need. вҖңI would be so relieved, and a lot easier to live with, if you do that.вҖқ Remember also to reward desired behavior after the fact. (continued on next page) From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5 (p. 2 of 2)
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5 (p. 2 of 2) (Stay) M indful Keep your focus on your goals. Maintain your position. DonвҖҷt be distracted. DonвҖҷt get off the topic. вҖңBroken recordвҖқ: Keep asking, saying no, or expressing your opinion over and over and over. Just keep replaying the same thing again...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5 (p. 2 of 2) (Stay) M indful Keep your focus on your goals. Maintain your position. DonвҖҷt be distracted. DonвҖҷt get off the topic. вҖңBroken recordвҖқ: Keep asking, saying no, or expressing your opinion over and over and over. Just keep replaying the same thing again and again. Ignore attacks: If another person attacks, threatens, or tries to change the subject, ignore the threats, comments, or attempts to divert you. Do not respond to attacks. Ignore distractions. Just keep making your point. вҖңI would still like a call.вҖқ A ppear confident Appear effective and competent. Use a confident voice tone and physical manner; make good eye contact. No stammering, whispering, staring at the floor, retreating. No saying, вҖңIвҖҷm not sure,вҖқ etc. N egotiate Be willing to give to get. Offer and ask for other solutions to the problem. Reduce your request. Say no, but offer to do something else or to solve the problem another way. Focus on what will work. вҖңHow about if you text me when you think you might be late?вҖқ Turn the tables: Turn the problem over to the other person. Ask for other solutions. вҖңWhat do you think we should do? . . . I canвҖҷt just stop worrying about you [or IвҖҷm not willing to].вҖқ Other ideas:

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5a
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5a Applying DEAR MAN Skills to a Difficult Current Interaction To turn around really difficult situations, focus the skills on the other personвҖҷs behavior right now. When other people have really good skills themselves, and keep refusing your legitimate requests o...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 5a Applying DEAR MAN Skills to a Difficult Current Interaction To turn around really difficult situations, focus the skills on the other personвҖҷs behavior right now. When other people have really good skills themselves, and keep refusing your legitimate requests or pestering you to do something you donвҖҷt want to do. Apply DEAR MAN Skills 1. Describe the current interaction. If the вҖңbroken recordвҖқ and ignoring donвҖҷt work, make a statement about what is happening between you and the person now, but without imputing motives. Example: вҖңYou keep asking me over and over, even though I have already said no several times,вҖқ or вҖңIt is hard to keep asking you to empty the dishwasher when it is your month to do it.вҖқ Not: вҖңYou obviously donвҖҷt want to hear what I am saying,вҖқ вҖңYou obviously donвҖҷt care about me,вҖқ вҖңWell, itвҖҷs obvious that what I have to say doesnвҖҷt matter to you,вҖқ вҖңObviously you think IвҖҷm stupid.вҖқ 2. Express feelings or opinions about the interaction. For instance, in the middle of an interaction that is not going well, you can express your feelings of discomfort in the situation. Example: вҖңI am sorry I cannot do what you want, but IвҖҷm finding it hard to keep discussing it,вҖқ or вҖңItвҖҷs becoming very uncomfortable for me to keep talking about this, since I canвҖҷt help it. I am starting to feel angry about it,вҖқ or вҖңIвҖҷm not sure you think this is important for you to do.вҖқ Not: вҖңI hate you!вҖқ, вҖңEvery time we talk about this, you get defensive,вҖқ вҖңStop patronizing me!вҖқ 3. Assert wishes in the situation. When another person is pestering you, you can ask him or her to stop it. When a person is refusing a request, you can suggest that you put the conversation off until another time. Give the other person a chance to think about it. Example: вҖңPlease donвҖҷt ask me again. My answer wonвҖҷt change,вҖқ or вҖңOK, letвҖҷs stop discussing this now and pick it up again sometime tomorrow,вҖқ or вҖңLetвҖҷs cool down for a while and then get together to figure out a solution.вҖқ Not: вҖңWould you shut up?вҖқ вҖңYou should do this!вҖқ, вҖңYou should really calm down and do whatвҖҷs right here.вҖқ 4. Reinforce. When you are saying no to someone who keeps asking, or when someone wonвҖҷt take your opinion seriously, suggest ending the conversation, since you arenвҖҷt going to change your mind anyway. When trying to get someone to do something for you, you can suggest that you will come up with a better offer later. Example: вҖңLetвҖҷs stop talking about this now. IвҖҷm not going to change my mind, and I think this is just going to get frustrating for both of us,вҖқ or вҖңOK, I can see you donвҖҷt want to do this, so letвҖҷs see if we can come up with something that will make you more willing to do it.вҖқ Not: вҖңIf you donвҖҷt do this for me, IвҖҷll never do anything for you ever again,вҖқ вҖңIf you keep asking me, IвҖҷll get a restraining order against you,вҖқ вҖңGosh, you must be a terrible person for not doing this/for asking me to do this.вҖқ From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheets 4, 5; pp. 174вҖ“175) В« Guidelines for Relationship Effectiveness: Keeping the Relationship (GIVE) A way to remember these skills is to remember the word GIVE (DEAR MAN, GIVE): (Be) Gentle (Act) Interested Validate (Be) (Use...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheets 4, 5; pp. 174вҖ“175) В« Guidelines for Relationship Effectiveness: Keeping the Relationship (GIVE) A way to remember these skills is to remember the word GIVE (DEAR MAN, GIVE): (Be) Gentle (Act) Interested Validate (Be) (Use an) Easy manner G entle No attacks: Be nice and respectful. No verbal or physical attacks. No hitting, clenching fists. No harassment of any kind. Express anger directly with words. No threats: If you have to describe painful consequences for not getting what you want, describe them calmly and without exaggerating. No вҖңmanipulativeвҖқ statements, no hidden threats. No вҖңIвҖҷll kill myself if you . . . вҖқ Tolerate a вҖңno.вҖқ Stay in the discussion even if it gets painful. Exit gracefully. No judging: No moralizing. No вҖңIf you were a good person, you would . . . вҖқ No вҖңYou should . . . вҖқ or вҖңYou shouldnвҖҷt . . . вҖқ Abandon blame. No sneering: No smirking, eye rolling, sucking teeth. No cutting off or walking away. No saying, вҖңThatвҖҷs stupid, donвҖҷt be sad,вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt care what you say.вҖқ (Act) Listen and appear interested in the other person. I nterested Listen to the other personвҖҷs point of view. Face the person; maintain eye contact; lean toward the person rather than away. DonвҖҷt interrupt or talk over the person. Be sensitive to the personвҖҷs wish to have the discussion at a later time. Be patient. V With words and actions, show that you understand the other personвҖҷs feelings and thoughts about the situation. See the world from the other personвҖҷs alidate point of view, and then say or act on what you see. вҖңI realize this is hard for you, and . . . вҖқ, вҖңI see that you are busy, and . . . вҖқ Go to a private place when the person is uncomfortable talking in a public place. (Use an) Use a little humor. E Smile. Ease the person along. Be light-Вӯhearted. Sweet-talk. Use a вҖңsoft sellвҖқ over a вҖңhard sell.вҖқ Be вҖңpolitical.вҖқ asy manner Leave your attitude at the door. Other ideas: From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6a
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6a Expanding the V in GIVE: Levels of Validation 1. пҒұ Pay Attention: Look interested in the other person instead of bored (no multitasking). 2. пҒұ Reflect Back: Say back what you heard the other person say or do, to be sure you understand exactly what the person is...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 6a Expanding the V in GIVE: Levels of Validation 1. пҒұ Pay Attention: Look interested in the other person instead of bored (no multitasking). 2. пҒұ Reflect Back: Say back what you heard the other person say or do, to be sure you understand exactly what the person is saying. No judgmental language or tone of voice! 3. пҒұ вҖңRead MindsвҖқ: Be sensitive to what is not being said by the other person. Pay attention to facial expressions, body language, what is happening, and what you know about the person already. Show you understand in words or by your actions. Check it out and make sure you are right. Let go if you are not. 4. пҒұ Understand: Look for how what the other person is feeling, thinking, or doing makes sense, based on the personвҖҷs past experiences, present situation, and/or current state of mind or physical condition (i.e., the causes). 5. пҒұ Acknowledge the Valid: Look for how the personвҖҷs feelings, thinking, or actions are valid responses because they fit current facts, or are understandable because they are a logical response to current facts. 6. пҒұ Show Equality: Be yourself! DonвҖҷt вҖңone-upвҖқ or вҖңone-downвҖқ the other person. Treat the other as an equal, not as fragile or incompetent. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 1 of 3)
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 1 of 3) (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 6; p. 176) В« Evaluating Options for Whether or How Intensely to Ask for Something or Say No Before asking for something or saying no to a request, you have to decide how intensely you want to hold your ground. O...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 1 of 3) (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 6; p. 176) В« Evaluating Options for Whether or How Intensely to Ask for Something or Say No Before asking for something or saying no to a request, you have to decide how intensely you want to hold your ground. Options range from very low intensity, where you are very flexible and accept the situation as it is, to very high intensity, where you try every skill you know to change the situation and get what you want. Options Low intensity (let go, give in) Asking Saying No Do what the other person wants without being DonвҖҷt ask; donвҖҷt hint. 1 asked. Hint indirectly; take no. 2 DonвҖҷt complain; do it cheerfully. Hint openly; take no. 3 Do it, even if youвҖҷre not cheerful about it. Ask tentatively; take no. 4 Do it, but show that youвҖҷd rather not. Ask gracefully, but take no. 5 Say youвҖҷd rather not, but do it gracefully. Ask confidently; take no. 6 Say no confidently, but reconsider. Ask confidently; resist no. 7 Say no confidently; resist saying yes. Ask firmly; resist no. 8 Say no firmly; resist saying yes. Ask firmly; insist; negotiate; keep Say no firmly; resist; negotiate; trying. keep trying. Ask and donвҖҷt take no for 10 DonвҖҷt do it. an answer. High intensity (stay firm) (continued on next page) From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 2 of 3)
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 2 of 3) Factors to Consider When deciding how firm or intense you want to be in asking or saying no, think about: 1. The other personвҖҷs or your own capability. 2. Your priorities. 3. The effect of your actions on your self-Вӯrespect. 4. Your or the otherвҖҷs mo...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 2 of 3) Factors to Consider When deciding how firm or intense you want to be in asking or saying no, think about: 1. The other personвҖҷs or your own capability. 2. Your priorities. 3. The effect of your actions on your self-Вӯrespect. 4. Your or the otherвҖҷs moral and legal rights in the situation. 5. Your authority over the person (or his or hers over you). 6. The type of relationship you have with the person. 7. The effect of your action on long- versus short-term goals. 8. The degree of give and take in your relationship. 9. Whether you have done your homework to prepare. 10. The timing of your request or refusal. 1. Capability: вҖўвҖў Is the person able to give you what you want? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Do you have what the person wants? If no, raise the intensity of no. 2. Priorities: вҖўвҖў Are your goals very important? Increase intensity. вҖўвҖў Is your relationship shaky? Consider reducing intensity. вҖўвҖў Is your self-Вӯrespect on the line? Intensity should fit your values. 3. Self-Вӯrespect: вҖўвҖў Do you usually do things for yourself? Are you careful to avoid acting helpless when you are not? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Will saying no make you feel bad about yourself, even when you are thinking about it wisely? If no, raise the intensity of no. 4. Rights: вҖўвҖў Is the person required by law or moral code to give you what you want? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Are you required to give the person what he or she is asking for? Would saying no violate the other personвҖҷs rights? If no, raise the intensity of no. 5. Authority: вҖўвҖў Are you responsible for directing the person or telling the person what to do? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Does the person have authority over you (e.g., your boss, your teacher)? And is what the person is asking within his or her authority? If no, raise the intensity of no. (continued on next page)

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 3 of 3)
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 3 of 3) 6. Relationship: вҖўвҖў Is what you want appropriate to the current relationship? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Is what the person is asking for appropriate to your current relationship? If no, raise the intensity of no. 7. Long-term вҖўвҖў Will...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 8 (p. 3 of 3) 6. Relationship: вҖўвҖў Is what you want appropriate to the current relationship? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Is what the person is asking for appropriate to your current relationship? If no, raise the intensity of no. 7. Long-term вҖўвҖў Will not asking for what you want keep the peace now but create problems in the versus long run? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. short-term вҖўвҖў Is giving in to keep the peace right now more important than the long-term welfare goals: of the relationship? Will you eventually regret or resent saying no? If no, raise the intensity of no. 8. GIve and take: вҖўвҖў What have you done for the person? Are you giving at least as much as you ask for? Are you willing to give if the person says yes? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Do you owe this person a favor? Does he or she do a lot for you? If no, raise the intensity of no. 9. Homework: вҖўвҖў Have you done your homework? Do you know all the facts you need to know to support your request? Are you clear about what you want? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Is the other personвҖҷs request clear? Do you know what you are agreeing to? If no, raise the intensity of no. 10. Timing: вҖўвҖў Is this a good time to ask? Is the person вҖңin the moodвҖқ for listening and paying attention to you? Are you catching the person when he or she is likely to say yes to your request? If yes, raise the intensity of asking. вҖўвҖў Is this a bad time to say no? Should you hold off answering for a while? If no, raise the intensity of no. Other factors:

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 9 (p. 2 of 2)
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 9 (p. 2 of 2) Are my emotions getting in the way of using my skills? Ask: вҖўвҖў Do I get too upset to use my skills? вҖўвҖў Are my emotions so high that I am over my skills breakdown point? Are worries, assumptions, and myths getting in my way? Ask: вҖўвҖў Are thoughts about...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 9 (p. 2 of 2) Are my emotions getting in the way of using my skills? Ask: вҖўвҖў Do I get too upset to use my skills? вҖўвҖў Are my emotions so high that I am over my skills breakdown point? Are worries, assumptions, and myths getting in my way? Ask: вҖўвҖў Are thoughts about bad consequences blocking my action? вҖңThey wonвҖҷt like me,вҖқ вҖңShe will think I am stupid.вҖқ 5 вҖўвҖў Are thoughts about not deserving things getting in my way? вҖңI am such a bad person I donвҖҷt deserve this.вҖқ вҖўвҖў Am I calling myself names that stop me from doing anything? вҖңI wonвҖҷt do it right,вҖқ вҖңIвҖҷll probably fall apart,вҖқ вҖңIвҖҷm so stupid.вҖқ вҖўвҖў Do I believe myths about interpersonal effectiveness? вҖңIf I make a request, this will show that I am a weak person,вҖқ вҖңOnly wimps have values.вҖқ Is the environment more powerful than my skills? Ask: вҖўвҖў Are the people who have what I want or need more powerful than I am? вҖўвҖў Are other people more in control of the situation than I am? вҖўвҖў Will others be threatened if I get what I want? вҖўвҖў Do others have reasons for not liking me if I get what I want? Other ideas:

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 11 (p. 1 of 2)
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 11 (p. 1 of 2) (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 8; p. 183) Finding and Getting People to Like You Remember: All Human Beings Are Lovable. But finding friends may take effort on your part. Look for People Who Are Close by You. Familiarity often leads to likin...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 11 (p. 1 of 2) (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 8; p. 183) Finding and Getting People to Like You Remember: All Human Beings Are Lovable. But finding friends may take effort on your part. Look for People Who Are Close by You. Familiarity often leads to liking and sometimes love. To find people you might like and who might like you, it is important to make sure that you are frequently around and visible to a group of people. Many people find friends who are classmates or members of groups they join, or who work at or go to the same places. Look for People Who Are Similar to You. We often make friends with people who share our interests and attitudes. Though always agreeing with someone will not make you more attractive to them, a lot of people are attracted to those who share the same important interests and attitudes, such as politics, lifestyle, morals. Work on Your Conversation Skills. Ask and respond to questions; respond with a little more info than requested. Make small talk; donвҖҷt underestimate the value of chit-chat. Self-Вӯdisclose skillfully; keep your self-Вӯdisclosure close to that of the other person. DonвҖҷt interrupt; donвҖҷt start talking just fractionally before or after someone else. Learn things to talk about: Watch others; read; increase your activities and experiences. Express Liking (Selectively). We often like the people we think like us. Express genuine liking for the other person. But donвҖҷt try to suck up to the other person or grovel. Find things to compliment that are not super-Вӯobvious. DonвҖҷt praise too much too often, and never use compliments to obtain favors. (continued on next page) Adapted from Linehan, M. M., & Egan, K. J. (1985). Asserting yourself. New York: Facts on File. chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 11 (p. 2 of 2) Join an Ongoing Group Conversation. If we wait for people to approach us, we may never have friends. Sometimes we must make the first move in finding friends. This often requires us to know how to tell if a group is open or closed, and then, when it is open, how to approach and join in the ongoing group. Figure Out If a Group Is Open or Closed. In open groups new members are welcome. In closed groups new members may not be welcome. Open Groups Closed Groups вҖўвҖў Everyone is standing somewhat apart. вҖўвҖў Everyone is standing close together. вҖўвҖў Members occasionally glance around the вҖўвҖў Members attend exclusively to each other. room. вҖўвҖў There is a very animated conversation with вҖўвҖў There are gaps in the conversation. few gaps. вҖўвҖў Members are talking about a topic of вҖўвҖў Members seem to be pairing off. general interest. Figure Out How to Join an Open Group Conversation. Ways of Joining an Open Group Potential Outcomes Move gradually closer to the group. It may not be clear from the slowness of your approach that you want to join them; it might even look as though you were creeping up and trying to eavesdrop! Offer to refill membersвҖҷ glasses/ That could be overdoing things a bit. What would you serve them food. do if they refused more food/drinks? Would it be clear enough that you wanted to join the group? Stand beside them and chip in on That might seem rude. They havenвҖҷt invited you to their conversation. join them, and anyway, what exactly are you going to say when you chip in? Go up and introduce yourself. IsnвҖҷt that overly formal? Having introduced yourself, then what do you say? Will they introduce themselves to you? WouldnвҖҷt you interrupt the conversation? Wait for a break in the This makes your intention clear and doesnвҖҷt conversation, stand beside a seem rude or interrupt the conversation; group friendly-Вӯlooking member of the members can then choose whether to introduce group and say something like themselves or not. вҖңMind if I join you?вҖқ

chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 12 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 9; p. 184) Mindfulness of Others Friendships last longer when we are mindful. Observe Pay attention with interest and curiosity to others around you. ВүВү Stop multitasking; focus on the people you are with. ВүВү Stay in the present rather than planning what to say next. ВүВү Let go of a focus on self, and focus on others around you. ВүВү Be open to new information about others. ВүВү Notice judgmental thoughts about others, and let them go. ВүВү Give up clinging to always being right. ВүВү Describe Replace judgmental words with descriptive words. ВүВү Avoid assuming or interpreting what other people think about you ВүВү without checking the facts. (Remember, no one has ever observed another personвҖҷs thoughts, motives, intentions, feelings, emotions, desires, or experiences.) Avoid questioning other peopleвҖҷs motives (unless you have very ВүВү good reasons to do so). Give others the benefit of the doubt. ВүВү Participate Throw yourself into interactions with others. ВүВү Go with the flow, rather than trying to control the flow. ВүВү Become one with group activities and conversations. ВүВү From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 13
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 13 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 10; pp. 185вҖ“186) Ending Relationships A destructive relationship has the quality of destroying or completely spoiling either the quality of the relationship or aspects of yourselfвҖ”such as your physical body and safety, you...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 13 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 10; pp. 185вҖ“186) Ending Relationships A destructive relationship has the quality of destroying or completely spoiling either the quality of the relationship or aspects of yourselfвҖ”such as your physical body and safety, your self-Вӯesteem or sense of integrity, your happiness or peace of mind, or your caring for the other person. An interfering relationship is one that blocks or makes difficult your pursuing goals that are important to you; your ability to enjoy life and do things you like doing; your relationships with other persons; or the welfare of others that you love. Decide to end relationships in Wise Mind, never in emotion mind. If the relationship is important and not destructive, and there is reason to hope it can be improved, try problem solving to repair a difficult relationship. cope ahead to troubleshoot and practice ending the relationship ahead of time. Be direct: Use the DEAR MAN GIVE FAST interpersonal effectiveness skills. Practice opposite action for love when you find you love the wrong person. Practice safety first! Before leaving a highly abusive or life-Вӯthreatening relationship, call a local domestic violence hotline or the toll-free National Domestic Violence Hotline (1-800-799-7233) for help with safety planning and a referral to a qualified professional. See also the International Directory of Domestic Violence Agencies (www.hotpeachpages.net). From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheets 11, 11a, 11b; pp. 189вҖ“191) How to Think and Act Dialectically 1. There is always more than one side to anything that exists. Look for both sides. ВүВү Ask Wise Mind: What am I missing? Where is the kernel of truth in the ot...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheets 11, 11a, 11b; pp. 189вҖ“191) How to Think and Act Dialectically 1. There is always more than one side to anything that exists. Look for both sides. ВүВү Ask Wise Mind: What am I missing? Where is the kernel of truth in the other side? ВүВү Let go of extremes: Change вҖңeither-orвҖқ to вҖңboth-and,вҖқ вҖңalwaysвҖқ or вҖңneverвҖқ to ВүВү вҖңsometimes.вҖқ Balance opposites: Validate both sides when you disagree, accept reality, and work to ВүВү change. Make lemonade out of lemons. ВүВү Embrace confusion: Enter the paradox of yes and no, or true and not true. ВүВү Play devilвҖҷs advocate: Argue each side of your own position with equal passion. ВүВү Use metaphors and storytelling to unstick and free the mind. ВүВү Other ways to see all sides of a situation: ВүВү 2. Be aware that you are connected. ВүВү Treat others as you want them to treat you. ВүВү Look for similarities among people instead of differences. ВүВү Notice the physical connections among all things. ВүВү Other ways to stay aware of connections: ВүВү 3. Embrace change. ВүВү Throw yourself into change: Allow it. Embrace it. ВүВү Practice radical acceptance of change when rules, circumstances, people, and ВүВү relationships change in ways you donвҖҷt like. Practice getting used to change: Make small changes to practice this (e.g., purposely ВүВү change where you sit, who you talk with, what route you take when going to a familiar place). Other ways to embrace change: ВүВү 4. Change is transactional: Remember that you affect your environment and your ВүВү environment affects you. Pay attention to your effect on others and how they affect you. ВүВү Practice letting go of blame by looking for how your own and othersвҖҷ behaviors are ВүВү caused by many interactions over time. Remind yourself that all things, including all behaviors, are caused. ВүВү Other ways to see transactions: ВүВү Note. Adapted from Miller, A. L., Rathus, J. H., & Linehan, M. M. (2007). Dialectical behavior therapy with suicidal adolescents. New York: Guilford Press. Copyright 2007 by The Guilford Press. Adapted by permission. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16a
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16a Examples of Opposite Sides That Can Both Be True ВүВү1. You can want to change and be doing the best you can, AND still need to do better, try harder, and be more motivated to change. ВүВү2. You are tough AND you are gentle. ВүВү3. You can be independent AND also wa...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16a Examples of Opposite Sides That Can Both Be True ВүВү1. You can want to change and be doing the best you can, AND still need to do better, try harder, and be more motivated to change. ВүВү2. You are tough AND you are gentle. ВүВү3. You can be independent AND also want help. (You can allow somebody else to be independent and also give them help.) ВүВү4. You can want to be alone AND also want to be connected to others. ВүВү5. You can share some things with others AND also keep some things private. ВүВү6. You can be by yourself AND still be connected to others. ВүВү7. You can be with others AND be lonely. ВүВү8. You can be a misfit in one group AND fit in perfectly in another group. (A tulip in a rose garden can also be a tulip in a tulip garden.) ВүВү9. You can accept yourself the way you are AND still want to change. (You can accept others as they are AND still want them to change.) 10. At times you need to both control AND tolerate your emotions. ВүВү 11. You may have a valid reason for believing what you believe, AND you may still be wrong or ВүВү incorrect. 12. Someone may have valid reasons for wanting something from you, AND you may have valid ВүВү reasons for saying no. 13. The day can be sunny, AND it can rain. ВүВү 14. You can be mad at somebody AND also love and respect the person. ВүВү 15. (You can be mad at yourself AND also love and respect yourself.) ВүВү 16. You can have a disagreement with somebody AND also be friends. ВүВү 17. You can disagree with the rules AND also follow the rules. ВүВү 18. You can understand why somebody is feeling or behaving in a certain way, AND also ВүВү disagree with his or her behavior and ask that it be changed. 19. Others: ВүВү Note. Adapted from Miller, A. L., Rathus, J. H., & Linehan, M. M. (2007). Dialectical behavior therapy with suicidal adolescents. New York: Guilford Press. Copyright 2007 by The Guilford Press. Adapted by permission. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 17
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 17 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 12; p. 192) Validation Validation Means: вҖўвҖў Finding the kernel of truth in another personвҖҷs perspective or situation; verifying the facts of a situation. вҖўвҖў Acknowledging that a personвҖҷs emotions, thoughts, and behaviors h...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 17 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 12; p. 192) Validation Validation Means: вҖўвҖў Finding the kernel of truth in another personвҖҷs perspective or situation; verifying the facts of a situation. вҖўвҖў Acknowledging that a personвҖҷs emotions, thoughts, and behaviors have causes and are therefore understandable. вҖўвҖў Not necessarily agreeing with the other person. вҖўвҖў Not validating what is actually invalid. Why Validate? вҖўвҖў It improves our relationships by showing we are listening and understand. вҖўвҖў It improves interpersonal effectiveness by reducing: 1. Pressure to prove who is right 2. Negative reactivity 3. Anger вҖўвҖў It makes problem solving, closeness, and support possible. вҖўвҖў Invalidation hurts. Important Things to Validate вҖўвҖў The valid (and only the valid). вҖўвҖў The facts of a situation. вҖўвҖў A personвҖҷs experiences, feelings/emotions, beliefs, opinions, or thoughts about something. вҖўвҖў Suffering and difficulties. Remember: вҖўвҖў Every invalid response makes sense in some way. вҖўвҖў Validation is not necessarily agreeing. вҖўвҖў Validation doesnвҖҷt mean you like it. вҖўвҖў Only validate the valid! Note. Adapted from Linehan, M. M. (1997). Validation and psychotherapy. In A. Bohart & L. Greenberg (Eds.), Empathy reconsidered: New directions in psychotherapy (pp. 353вҖ“392). Washington, DC: American Psychological Association. Copyright 1997 by the American Psychological Association. Adapted by permission. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 18
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 18 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 12; p. 192) A вҖңHow ToвҖқ Guide to Validation 1. пҒұ Pay Attention: Look interested, listen, and observe. No multitasking. Make eye contact. Stay focused. Nod occasionally. Respond with your face (e.g., smile at happy statement...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 18 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 12; p. 192) A вҖңHow ToвҖқ Guide to Validation 1. пҒұ Pay Attention: Look interested, listen, and observe. No multitasking. Make eye contact. Stay focused. Nod occasionally. Respond with your face (e.g., smile at happy statements; look concerned when hearing something painful). 2. пҒұ Reflect Back: Say back what you heard or observed to be sure you actually understand what the person is saying. No judgmental language or voice tone! Try to really вҖңgetвҖқ what the person feels or thinks. Have an open mind. (No disagreeing, criticizing, or trying to change the personвҖҷs mind or goals.) Use a voice tone that allows the other person to correct you . . . and check the facts! Example: вҖңSo you are mad at me because you think I lied just to get back at you. Did I get it right?вҖқ 3. пҒұ вҖңRead MindsвҖқ: Be sensitive to what is not being said by the other person. Pay attention to facial expressions, body language, what is happening, and what you know about the person already. Show that you understand in words or by your actions. Be open to correction. Example: When you are asking a friend for a ride at the end of a long day and the person slumps down, say, вҖңYou look really tired. Let me look for someone else.вҖқ 4. пҒұ Understand: Look for how the other person feels, is thinking, or if he or she is making sense, given the personвҖҷs history, state of mind or body, or current events (i.e. the causes)вҖ”even if you donвҖҷt approve of the personвҖҷs behavior, or if his or her belief is incorrect. Say вҖңIt makes sense that you . . . because . . . вҖқ Example: If you sent a party invitation to the wrong address, say, вҖңI can see why you thought I might be excluding you on purpose.вҖқ 5. пҒұ Acknowledge the valid: Show that you see that the personвҖҷs thoughts, feelings, or actions are valid, given current reality and facts. Act as if the personвҖҷs behavior is valid. Example: If you are criticized for not taking out the garbage on your day, admit that it is your day and take it out. If people present a problem, help them solve it (unless they just want to be heard). If people are hungry, give them food. Acknowledge the effort a person is making. 6. пҒұ Show Equality: Be yourself! DonвҖҷt вҖңone-upвҖқ or вҖңone-downвҖқ the other person. Treat the other as an equal, not as fragile or incompetent. Example: Be willing to admit mistakes. If someone introduces him- or herself by first name, introduce yourself by your first name. Ask other people for their opinions. Give up being defensive. Be careful in giving advice or telling someone what to do if you are not asked or required to do so. Even then, remember you could be wrong. Note. Adapted from Linehan, M. M. (1997). Validation and psychotherapy. In A. Bohart & L. Greenberg (Eds.), Empathy reconsidered: New directions in psychotherapy (pp. 353вҖ“392). Washington, DC: American Psychological Association. Copyright 1997 by the American Psychological Association. Adapted by permission. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

ВүВү Check them out with someone you can trust to validate the valid. Acknowledge when your responses donвҖҷt make sense and are not ВүВү valid. Work to...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 19 (p. 2 of 2) Be Nondefensive and Check the Facts Check all the facts to see if your responses are valid or invalid. ВүВү Check them out with someone you can trust to validate the valid. Acknowledge when your responses donвҖҷt make sense and are not ВүВү valid. Work to change invalid thinking, comments, or actions. (Also, stop ВүВү blaming. It rarely helps a situation.) Drop judgmental self-Вӯstatements. (Practice opposite action.) ВүВү Remind yourself that all behavior is caused and that you are doing ВүВү your best. Be compassionate toward yourself. Practice self-Вӯsoothing. ВүВү Admit that it hurts to be invalidated by others, even if they are right. ВүВү Acknowledge when your reactions make sense and are valid in a ВүВү situation. Remember that being invalidated, even when your response is ВүВү actually valid, is rarely a complete catastrophe. Describe your experiences and actions in a supportive environment. ВүВү Grieve traumatic invalidation and the harm it created. ВүВү Practice radical acceptance of the invalidating person. ВүВү Validate Yourself Exactly the Way You Would Validate Someone Else

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 20
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 20 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 14; p. 194) Strategies for Increasing the Probability of Behaviors You Want Describe behaviors for yourself or others that you would like to start or increase: Reinforcer = A consequence that increases frequency of a behav...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 20 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 14; p. 194) Strategies for Increasing the Probability of Behaviors You Want Describe behaviors for yourself or others that you would like to start or increase: Reinforcer = A consequence that increases frequency of a behavior. Positive reinforcement = positive consequences (i.e., reward). Behavior is increased by consequences a person wants, likes, or will work to get. Examples: Negative reinforcement = removal of negative events (i.e., relief). Behavior is increased by consequences that stop or reduce something negative. Examples: Shaping = Reinforcing small steps toward the behavior you want. вҖўвҖў Reinforce small steps that lead toward the goal. вҖўвҖў As new behavior stabilizes, require a little bit more before reinforcing. вҖўвҖў Continue until you reach the goal behavior. Examples of steps to a goal behavior: Timing counts. вҖўвҖў Reinforce behavior immediately after it occurs. вҖўвҖў When shaping new behavior, at first reinforce every instance of the behavior. вҖўвҖў Once behavior is established, gradually start to reinforce only some of the time. CAUTION: When you vary reinforcement, behavior becomes very hard to stop. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 21
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 21 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 15; p. 195) Strategies for Decreasing or Stopping Unwanted Behaviors Extinction = Stopping an ongoing reinforcement of behavior. Extinction leads first to a burst of behavior, and then to a decrease in behavior. Examples:...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 21 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 15; p. 195) Strategies for Decreasing or Stopping Unwanted Behaviors Extinction = Stopping an ongoing reinforcement of behavior. Extinction leads first to a burst of behavior, and then to a decrease in behavior. Examples: Satiation = Providing relief or what is wanted before the behavior occurs. Satiation reduces motivation for behavior and thus decreases its frequency. Examples: Punishment = An aversive consequence that decreases a behavior. Behavior is decreased by consequences the person dislikes or will work to avoid. Examples: Behavior is decreased by consequences that stop or reduce something positive. Examples: Behavior is decreased when something the person wants is withheld until harmful effects of problem behaviors are corrected and overcorrected. Examples: вҖўвҖў Be sure that punishment is specific, is time-Вӯlimited, and fits the вҖңcrime.вҖқ вҖўвҖў Avoid a punitive tone; let the consequence do the work. вҖўвҖў If a natural punishment occurs, donвҖҷt undo it. DonвҖҷt add arbitrary punishment. Be sure to reinforce alternative behavior to replace behavior you want stopped. вҖўвҖў Extinction and punishment weaken or suppress behavior, but do not eliminate it. вҖўвҖў Extinction and punishment do not teach new behavior. вҖўвҖў To keep a behavior from resurfacing, reinforce an alternative behavior. вҖўвҖў Punishment works only when the punisher is (or is likely to be) present. вҖўвҖў Punishment leads to avoidance of the person punishing. Note. Adapted from Miller, A. L., Rathus, J. H., & Linehan, M. M. (2007). Dialectical behavior therapy with suicidal adolescents. New York: Guilford Press. Copyright 2007 by The Guilford Press. chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 1
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 1 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 1; p. 117) Pros and Cons of Using Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Use this sheet to figure out the advantages and disadvantages to you of using interpersonal effectiveness skills (i.e., act...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 1 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 1; p. 117) Pros and Cons of Using Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Use this sheet to figure out the advantages and disadvantages to you of using interpersonal effectiveness skills (i.e., acting skillfully) to get what you want. The idea here is to figure out what is the most effective way for you to get what you want. Remember, this is about your goals, not someone elseвҖҷs goals. Describe the interpersonal situation: Describe your goal in this situation: Make a list of the pros and cons of acting skillfully by using interpersonal effectiveness skills. Make another list of the pros and cons for using power tactics to get what you want. Make a third list of pros and cons for giving in or acting passively in the situation. Check the facts to be sure that you are correct in your assessment of advantages and disadvantages. Write on the back of this sheet if you need more room. Using Skills Demanding, Attacking, Stonewalling Giving In, Acting Passively Pros Using Skills Demanding, Attacking, Stonewalling Giving In, Acting Passively Cons What did you decide to do in this situation? Is this the best decision (in Wise Mind)? From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this worksheet is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 2 (p. 1 of 2)
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 2 (p. 1 of 2) (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2a; p. 119) Challenging Myths in the Way of Obtaining Objectives Challenging Myths in the Way of Objectives Effectiveness Due Date: Name: Week Starting: For each myth, write down a challenge that makes sense to...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 2 (p. 1 of 2) (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2a; p. 119) Challenging Myths in the Way of Obtaining Objectives Challenging Myths in the Way of Objectives Effectiveness Due Date: Name: Week Starting: For each myth, write down a challenge that makes sense to you. 1. I donвҖҷt deserve to get what I want or need. Challenge: 2. If I make a request, this will show that IвҖҷm a very weak person. Challenge: 3. I have to know whether a person is going to say yes before I make a request. Challenge: 4. If I ask for something or say no, I canвҖҷt stand it if someone gets upset with me. Challenge: 5. If they say no, it will kill me. Challenge: 6. Making requests is a really pushy (bad, self-Вӯcentered, selfish, etc.) thing to do. Challenge: 7. Saying no to a request is always a selfish thing to do. Challenge: 8. I should be willing to sacrifice my own needs for others. Challenge: 9. I must be really inadequate if I canвҖҷt fix this myself. Challenge: 10. Obviously, the problem is just in my head. If I would just think differently, I wouldnвҖҷt have to bother everybody else. Challenge: 11. If I donвҖҷt have what I want or need, it doesnвҖҷt make any difference; I donвҖҷt care, really. Challenge: 12. Skillfulness is a sign of weakness. Challenge: Other myth: Challenge: Other myth: Challenge: (continued on next page) From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. I shouldnвҖҷt have to ask (say no); they should know what I want (and do it). Challenge: 14. They should have known that their behavior would hurt my feelings; I shouldnвҖҷt have to tell them. Challenge: 15. I shouldnвҖҷt have to negotiate or work at getting what I want. Challenge: 16. Other people should be willing to do more for my needs. Challenge: 17. Other people should like, approve of, and support me. Challenge: 18. They donвҖҷt deserve my being skillful or treating them well. Challenge: 19. Getting what I want when I want it is most important. Challenge: 20. I shouldnвҖҷt be fair, kind, courteous, or respectful if others are not so toward me. Challenge: 21. Revenge will feel so good; it will be worth any negative consequences. Challenge: 22. Only wimps have values. Challenge: 23. Everybody lies. Challenge: 24. Getting what I want or need is more important than how I get it; the ends really do justify the means. chunks.heading: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 3
chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 3 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 4; p. 124) Clarifying Priorities in Interpersonal Situations Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Use this sheet to figure out your goals and priorities in any situation that creates a problem for you. Examples include situations...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 3 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 4; p. 124) Clarifying Priorities in Interpersonal Situations Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Use this sheet to figure out your goals and priorities in any situation that creates a problem for you. Examples include situations where (1) your rights or wishes are not being respected; (2) you want someone to do or change something or give you something; (3) you want or need to say no or resist pressure to do something; (4) you want to get your position or point of view taken seriously; (5) there is conflict with another person; or (6) you want to improve your relationship with someone. Observe and describe in writing as close in time to the situation as possible. Write on the back of this sheet if you need more room. Prompting event for my problem: Who did what to whom? What led up to what? What is it about this situation that is a problem for me? Remember to check the facts! My wants and desires in this situation: Objectives: What specific results do I want? What do I want this person to do, stop or accept? Relationship: How do I want the other person to feel and think about me because of how I handle the interaction (whether or not I get what I want from the other person)? Self-ВӯRespect: How do I want to feel or think about myself because of how I handle the interaction (whether or not I get what I want from the other person)? My priorities in this situation: Rate priorities 1 (most important), 2 (second most important), or 3 (least important). Objectives Relationship Self-Вӯrespect Imbalances and conflicts in priorities that make it hard to be effective in this situation: From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this worksheet is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

Practice saying your вҖңlinesвҖқ out l...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 4 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handouts 5, 6, 7; pp. 125вҖ“130) Writing Out Interpersonal Effectiveness Scripts Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Fill out this sheet before you practice your DEAR MAN, GIVE FAST interpersonal skills. Practice saying your вҖңlinesвҖқ out loud, and also in your mind. Use the вҖңcope aheadвҖқ skills (Emotion Regulation Handout 19). Write on the back of this sheet if you need more room. Prompting event for my problem: Who did what to whom? What led up to what? Objectives in Situation (What results I want): Relationship Issue (How I want the other person to feel about me): Self-ВӯRespect Issue (How I want to feel about myself): Script Ideas for DEAR MAN, GIVE FAST 1. Describe situation. 2. Express feelings/opinions. 3. Assert request (or say no) directly (circle the part you will use later in вҖңbroken recordвҖқ to stay Mindful if you need it). 4. Reinforcing comments to make. 5. Mindful and Appearing confident comments to make (if needed). 6 Negotiating comments to make, plus turn-the-table comments (if needed). 7. Validating comments. 8. Easy manner comments. Write on the back side all the things you want to avoid doing and saying. From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. ВӯPermission to photocopy or download and print this worksheet is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use or for use with clients.

chunks.preview: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 5 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handouts 5, 6, 7; pp. 125вҖ“130) Tracking Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Use Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Fill out this sheet whenever you practice your interpersonal skills and whenever you have an opportunity to practice, eve...
chunks.text: Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 5 (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handouts 5, 6, 7; pp. 125вҖ“130) Tracking Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Use Due Date: Name: Week Starting: Fill out this sheet whenever you practice your interpersonal skills and whenever you have an opportunity to practice, even if you donвҖҷt (or almost donвҖҷt) do anything to practice. Write on the back of this sheet if you need more room. Prompting event for my problem: Who did what to whom? What led up to what? Objectives in Situation (What results I want): Relationship Issue (How I want the other person to feel about me): Self-ВӯRespect Issue (How I want to feel about myself): My priorities in this situation: Rate priorities 1 (most important), 2 (second most important), or 3 (least important). Objectives Relationship Self-Вӯrespect Imbalances and conflicts in priorities that made it hard to be effective in this situation: What I said or did in the situation: (Describe and check below.) DEAR MAN (Getting what I want): Described situation? Mindful? Expressed feelings/opinions? Broken record? Asserted? Ignored attacks? Reinforced? Appeared confident? Negotiated? GIVE (Keeping the relationship): Gentle? Interested? No threats? Validated? No attacks? Easy manner? No judgments? FAST (Keeping my respect for myself): Fair? Stuck to values? (No) Apologies? Truthful? How effective was the interaction? From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition, by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan.
